EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass was gradually retreating from Arkansas on the 13th, but precipitation moved in from the west before the cold air could exit. In most parts of Arkansas, precipitation began as snow. Then, as warmer air moved in during the day, the snow changed to a mixture of sleet and freezing rain. This occurred during the morning in southern and central sections, and during the afternoon in the north. Finally, the wintry mix changed over to rain as warmer air continued to invade the state. This changeover occurred during the morning in the south, around midday in central sections, and during the afternoon or early evening in the north. Snowfall totals over the southern half of the state were well less than an inch. Over the northern half, most snow accumulations were in the 1 to 3 inch range. However, totals to 4 inches were reported in the higher elevations of Newton County south of Jasper, and in northern Baxter County. Freezing rain amounts ranged from a trace to around .10 inch.

Low humidities and gusty winds made conditions favorable for wildfires late in February. On the 26th, a wildfire 2 miles west of Vick in Bradley County burned 124 acres.
Low humidities and gusty winds made conditions favorable for wildfires late in February. On the 29th, a wildfire 4 miles northeast of Evening Shade in Sharp County burned 150 acres.

A strong shortwave and associated cold front/dry line merger helped to generate showers and thunderstorms across portions of Southern Arkansas and Northern Louisiana during the evening hours of October 17th.  While deep layer moisture was not available for this system, frontal forcing proved sufficient for a few severe thunderstorms across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed across the city of El Dorado, Arkansas.

A strong storm system translated from the Pacific Northwest across the Southern Rockies and into the Southern Plains from the 23rd through the 25th. Rain began across west central Arkansas during the early morning hours of the 25th as this system approached and then transitioned to light freezing rain and a little sleet. The precipitation changed over to snow during the early afternoon of the 25th and fell heavily for several hours before tapering off late in the evening. A swath of heavy snow from four to six inches occurred across west central Arkansas.

A strong upper level disturbance and associated cold front moved through the Mid-South during the evening hours of October 17, 2012. The cold front interacted with a moist and unstable airmass in place over the region and a line of showers and thunderstorms developed over Eastern Arkansas and then swept east across the Mid-South. Strong winds aloft aided in the development of the line of thunderstorms some of which became severe and produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked trees down on County Road 300 near Hornor.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado developed near Highway 96 north of Union Road where it damaged a permanent home and trees. It moved northeast uprooting or snapping numerous hardwood and softwood trees and damaging chicken houses and outbuildings. The tornado damaged the sides and roof of a fire station on County Road 68, tore the roof off a double wide mobile home north of County Road 68, then destroyed a double wide mobile home and tore the roofs off of three chicken houses before moving into a wooded area. Based on this damage, maximum estimated wind in the tornado was 100 to 110 mph.

A shortwave trough rapidly traversed east across Oklahoma, Arkansas, and extreme Northeast Texas on February 1st, just ahead of a weak cold front that extended from North Texas, into Southeast Oklahoma and Western Arkansas.  The air mass south of the front over Northeast Texas, Southwest Arkansas, and extreme Northern Louisiana destabilized sufficiently as temperatures warmed well into the 70s, within a very moist air mass in place.  Cold temperatures and strong instability aloft ahead of this shortwave trough resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms developing over the region, some of which produced marginally severe to at times, severe hail.  These storms exited the region during the late afternoon hours, although additional widely scattered thunderstorms redeveloped during the mid-evening hours over Northwest and Northcentral Louisiana, and again produced a couple of reports of marginally severe hail in and near Monroe, LA.  These storms diminished during the late evening, as they moved away from the better forcing near the front as it drifted into extreme Northern Louisiana and Deep East Texas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable airmass developed across the Four State region during the afternoon and evening hours of December 12th. A potent upper level storm system moved out of the Inter Mountain West and into the Southern Plains during the afternoon as well. A warm front moved north of the region allowing for dewpoint temperatures well into the 60s across the entire region. Shear was very strong ahead of this upper level storm system with nearly 70 knots of deep layer shear. The wind profile however was unidirectional which given the instability in place, provided the necessary ingredients for a squall line to develop. Storms originally developed across North Central Texas and Southern Oklahoma along a prefrontal surface trough. The broken line of storms increased in coverage and intensity with the squall line moving through the entire Four State Region during the late night hours of the 19th and early morning hours of the 20th. Wind damage was widespread across the entire Four State Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed near Hwy. 53 and Walker Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The arrival of a cold front triggered scattered thunderstorms on the afternoon and evening of the 3rd. Hail fell in a number of locations, but most of it was small. In only one instance was the hail large enough to be considered severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell in west Little Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The day of the 22nd began cloudy, chilly, and foggy across most of Arkansas. During the day, however, much warmer air surged northward across much of the state. In the evening, a cold front plowed into the warmth and moisture, triggering severe thunderstorms and seven tornadoes.

A deep upper level trough across the western plains lifted north and east towards the region during the afternoon and overnight hours of April 3rd and morning hours of April 4th. Deep layer shear supported the development of super cell thunderstorms across North Central Texas during the afternoon hours of April 3rd and these storms moved into the region, producing strong and damaging wind gusts, large hail and a few tornadoes across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to penny sized hail reported in Lewisville, Arkansas near the intersection of Hwy. 82 and Hwy. 29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level low pressure system that produced severe thunderstorms across the southern half of Northeast Texas into much of Northern Louisiana produced widespread rain across all of Southwest Arkansas during Christmas Day.  However, much colder arctic air moved into the Middle Red River Valley during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 25th which allowed the rain to change over to snow very quickly. Lighter amounts of accumulating snow was reported across a few counties in Southwest Arkansas. This heavy wet snow resulted in a few minor traffic accidents due to snow accumulating on some roadways and overpasses. The following were some snowfall accumulations across Northeast Texas: 2 inches near Texarkana, 3 inches near Prescott.

An upper-level low pressure system touched off thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 5th. Some of the storms produced hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of hail, ranging from .25 to .75 inch in diameter, in the Benton area. Hail covered the ground in some places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds occurred in central Arkansas on the 18th, following the passage of a cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level storm system moved across Southern Oklahoma into Northern Texas during the evening hours of January 22nd.  A cold front preceded the storm system and the atmosphere became moderately unstable ahead of the front across Southern Arkansas.  Thunderstorms developed across Southwest Arkansas during the evening along and ahead of the cold front.  These storms produced strong tornadoes across Central and Northeast Arkansas but across Southwest Arkansas, strong and damaging wind gusts were the primary hazard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs and other debris was scattered across Hwy. 371 south of Rosston, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS developed across Southeast Texas during the overnight and early morning hours of April 2nd. This upper level disturbance rounded the base of an upper level trough across Southeast Texas and moved northeastward into Northeast Texas, Northern Louisiana and Southern Arkansas during the late morning and afternoon hours of April 2nd. The atmosphere ahead of this line of thunderstorms became very unstable by late morning with CAPE values approaching 4000 J/Kg. Very steep lapse rates were also present such that large hail and damaging thunderstorm wind gusts were the primary threat with these storms. Widespread tree damage was reported across all of Northeast Texas, Northern Louisiana and Southern Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were snapped and uprooted on Old Union Road in the eastern portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Southern and Southwest Arkansas during the late afternoon and early evening hours of March 8th.  Pressure rises were very strong behind this frontal boundary, resulting in very strong post-frontal gradient winds.  Winds were sustained near 20 to 25 mph with gusts as high as 45 mph across the southern half of the state.  These winds downed a tree on a home in Miller County, Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong gradient winds downed a tree which fell on a home in Texarkana, Arkansas.  There were no report of injuries received.

The wind profile however was unidirectional which given the instability in place, provided the necessary ingredients for a squall line to develop. Storms originally developed across North Central Texas and Southern Oklahoma along a prefrontal surface trough. The broken line of storms increased in coverage and intensity with the squall line moving through the entire Four State Region during the late night hours of the 19th and early morning hours of the 20th. Wind damage was widespread across the entire Four State Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed around El Dorado including one tree on a home just north of town.  There were no reports of injuries.

A vigorous upper level storm system moved across Southern Oklahoma into Northern Texas during the evening hours of January 22nd.  A cold front preceded the storm system and the atmosphere became moderately unstable ahead of the front across Southern Arkansas.  Thunderstorms developed across Southwest Arkansas during the evening along and ahead of the cold front.  These storms produced strong tornadoes across Central and Northeast Arkansas but across Southwest Arkansas, strong and damaging wind gusts were the primary hazard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of two damaged chicken houses on CR 1310 between Ozan and Nashville, Arkansas.

This activity produced some large hail with a few of the storms as they moved southeast during the late afternoon into the late evening hours.  Across Northwest Louisiana...this activity persisted into the predawn hours of April 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell 6 miles northeast of Nashville, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beneficial rain during the month of November improved drought conditions across Eastern Arkansas. In fact, severe (D2) drought conditions improved to moderate (D1) conditions everywhere except Randolph County where severe (D2) conditions continued.  Lake and river levels improved due to the rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions improved to moderate (D1) conditions across the county.

Rainfall during October was less than normal in most of Arkansas, so improvements in the drought were limited. The greatest change occurred in parts of northern Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of October, severe drought covered much of the northern part of Pulaski County, as well as the extreme south, while extreme drought existed elsewhere. Conditions were unchanged at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass was gradually retreating from Arkansas on the 13th, but precipitation moved in from the west before the cold air could exit. In most parts of Arkansas, precipitation began as snow. Then, as warmer air moved in during the day, the snow changed to a mixture of sleet and freezing rain. This occurred during the morning in southern and central sections, and during the afternoon in the north. Finally, the wintry mix changed over to rain as warmer air continued to invade the state. This changeover occurred during the morning in the south, around midday in central sections, and during the afternoon or early evening in the north. Snowfall totals over the southern half of the state were well less than an inch. Over the northern half, most snow accumulations were in the 1 to 3 inch range. However, totals to 4 inches were reported in the higher elevations of Newton County south of Jasper, and in northern Baxter County. Freezing rain amounts ranged from a trace to around .10 inch.

An MCS developed across Southeast Texas during the overnight and early morning hours of April 2nd. This upper level disturbance rounded the base of an upper level trough across Southeast Texas and moved northeastward into Northeast Texas, Northern Louisiana and Southern Arkansas during the late morning and afternoon hours of April 2nd. The atmosphere ahead of this line of thunderstorms became very unstable by late morning with CAPE values approaching 4000 J/Kg. Very steep lapse rates were also present such that large hail and damaging thunderstorm wind gusts were the primary threat with these storms. Widespread tree damage was reported across all of Northeast Texas, Northern Louisiana and Southern Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed in Alleene, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front, accompanied by numerous thunderstorms, moved through Arkansas on the 4th. The storms brought very frequent lightning, with more than 11,000 cloud-to-ground lightning strikes recorded in the state, along with some locally heavy rain, some street flooding, and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding occurred in the Pine Bluff area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing fog developed over the Arkansas River in central Arkansas on the morning of the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing fog caused havoc during the morning commute on the Interstate 440 bridge over the Arkansas River.  Numerous accidents occurred on the icy road, blocking the entire interstate at times.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a large pine tree and then jumped to an adjacent house, setting it on fire. The house was a total loss, as was a motorcycle parked in front of the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Beneficial rain during the month of November improved drought conditions across Eastern Arkansas. In fact, severe (D2) drought conditions improved to moderate (D1) conditions everywhere except Randolph County where severe (D2) conditions continued.  Lake and river levels improved due to the rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions improved to moderate (D1) conditions across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable airmass developed across the Four State region during the afternoon and evening hours of December 12th. A potent upper level storm system moved out of the Inter Mountain West and into the Southern Plains during the afternoon as well. A warm front moved north of the region allowing for dewpoint temperatures well into the 60s across the entire region. Shear was very strong ahead of this upper level storm system with nearly 70 knots of deep layer shear. The wind profile however was unidirectional which given the instability in place, provided the necessary ingredients for a squall line to develop. Storms originally developed across North Central Texas and Southern Oklahoma along a prefrontal surface trough. The broken line of storms increased in coverage and intensity with the squall line moving through the entire Four State Region during the late night hours of the 19th and early morning hours of the 20th. Wind damage was widespread across the entire Four State Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and powerlines were downed throughout the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass was gradually retreating from Arkansas on the 13th, but precipitation moved in from the west before the cold air could exit. In most parts of Arkansas, precipitation began as snow. Then, as warmer air moved in during the day, the snow changed to a mixture of sleet and freezing rain. This occurred during the morning in southern and central sections, and during the afternoon in the north. Finally, the wintry mix changed over to rain as warmer air continued to invade the state. This changeover occurred during the morning in the south, around midday in central sections, and during the afternoon or early evening in the north. Snowfall totals over the southern half of the state were well less than an inch. Over the northern half, most snow accumulations were in the 1 to 3 inch range. However, totals to 4 inches were reported in the higher elevations of Newton County south of Jasper, and in northern Baxter County. Freezing rain amounts ranged from a trace to around .10 inch.

A deep upper level trough across the western plains lifted north and east towards the region during the afternoon and overnight hours of April 3rd and morning hours of April 4th. Deep layer shear supported the development of super cell thunderstorms across North Central Texas during the afternoon hours of April 3rd and these storms moved into the region, producing strong and damaging wind gusts, large hail and a few tornadoes across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough rapidly traversed east across Oklahoma, Arkansas, and extreme Northeast Texas on February 1st, just ahead of a weak cold front that extended from North Texas, into Southeast Oklahoma and Western Arkansas.  The air mass south of the front over Northeast Texas, Southwest Arkansas, and extreme Northern Louisiana destabilized sufficiently as temperatures warmed well into the 70s, within a very moist air mass in place.  Cold temperatures and strong instability aloft ahead of this shortwave trough resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms developing over the region, some of which produced marginally severe to at times, severe hail.  These storms exited the region during the late afternoon hours, although additional widely scattered thunderstorms redeveloped during the mid-evening hours over Northwest and Northcentral Louisiana, and again produced a couple of reports of marginally severe hail in and near Monroe, LA.  These storms diminished during the late evening, as they moved away from the better forcing near the front as it drifted into extreme Northern Louisiana and Deep East Texas.
Rainfall during October was less than normal in most of Arkansas, so improvements in the drought were limited. The greatest change occurred in parts of northern Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of October, extreme drought covered most of Lonoke County. In the south, severe drought prevailed, tapering off to only abnormally dry in the extreme south. Conditions were unchanged at the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level trough moved across the Central Plains, pushing a strong cold front across North Texas and Central Oklahoma and through the Four State Region. Storms began developing ahead of the front along a pre-frontal trough axis across parts of East Texas and slowly propagated eastward. The front eventually merged with the pre-frontal trough, as the storms intensified and continued to push across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed along CR. 13 near Genoa, Arkansas.

Windy conditions prevailed ahead of an approaching cold front, with gusts of 30 to 40 mph common. As the front moved in, thunderstorms developed. Isolated severe weather, including one tornado, resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two men took a kayak out onto Crown Lake in the city of Horseshoe Bend. The closest observation stations indicated winds were gusting between 25 and 35 mph, and reports indicated 3 foot waves on the lake. The kayak capsized, throwing the men into the lake. One reached shore, but the other man, 25 years old, drowned. His body was recovered the next day.

A warm front moved north of the region allowing for dewpoint temperatures well into the 60s across the entire region. Shear was very strong ahead of this upper level storm system with nearly 70 knots of deep layer shear. The wind profile however was unidirectional which given the instability in place, provided the necessary ingredients for a squall line to develop. Storms originally developed across North Central Texas and Southern Oklahoma along a prefrontal surface trough. The broken line of storms increased in coverage and intensity with the squall line moving through the entire Four State Region during the late night hours of the 19th and early morning hours of the 20th. Wind damage was widespread across the entire Four State Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines were downed through the entire county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass was gradually retreating from Arkansas on the 13th, but precipitation moved in from the west before the cold air could exit. In most parts of Arkansas, precipitation began as snow. Then, as warmer air moved in during the day, the snow changed to a mixture of sleet and freezing rain. This occurred during the morning in southern and central sections, and during the afternoon in the north. Finally, the wintry mix changed over to rain as warmer air continued to invade the state. This changeover occurred during the morning in the south, around midday in central sections, and during the afternoon or early evening in the north. Snowfall totals over the southern half of the state were well less than an inch. Over the northern half, most snow accumulations were in the 1 to 3 inch range. However, totals to 4 inches were reported in the higher elevations of Newton County south of Jasper, and in northern Baxter County. Freezing rain amounts ranged from a trace to around .10 inch.

Near normal rainfall during the month of December kept drought conditions status quo across Eastern Arkansas. As a result...severe (D2) drought conditions continued across Randolph County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions continued across Randolph County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough rapidly traversed east across Oklahoma, Arkansas, and extreme Northeast Texas on February 1st, just ahead of a weak cold front that extended from North Texas, into Southeast Oklahoma and Western Arkansas.  The air mass south of the front over Northeast Texas, Southwest Arkansas, and extreme Northern Louisiana destabilized sufficiently as temperatures warmed well into the 70s, within a very moist air mass in place.  Cold temperatures and strong instability aloft ahead of this shortwave trough resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms developing over the region, some of which produced marginally severe to at times, severe hail.  These storms exited the region during the late afternoon hours, although additional widely scattered thunderstorms redeveloped during the mid-evening hours over Northwest and Northcentral Louisiana, and again produced a couple of reports of marginally severe hail in and near Monroe, LA.  These storms diminished during the late evening, as they moved away from the better forcing near the front as it drifted into extreme Northern Louisiana and Deep East Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail reported near DeQueen Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough across the western plains lifted north and east towards the region during the afternoon and overnight hours of April 3rd and morning hours of April 4th. Deep layer shear supported the development of super cell thunderstorms across North Central Texas during the afternoon hours of April 3rd and these storms moved into the region, producing strong and damaging wind gusts, large hail and a few tornadoes across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed on Aurelle Road in Strong, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the last week in June, almost all observing sites rose to at least 100 degrees. The hottest temperature was 111 degrees at the forestry reporting station in Evening Shade. Batesville Lock and Dam reached 110. Readings of 109 were recorded at Mountain Home, Blanchard Springs, Guy, Russellville, and Searcy. Stations that set records for the hottest day ever recorded in the month of June included: Dardanelle, Gilbert, Marshall, Star City/Cane Creek State Park, Batesville Airport, Hot Springs Airport, Harrison, Little Rock, Monticello, Little Rock Air Force Base, North Little Rock, Russellville, Batesville Lock and Dam, Greers Ferry Dam, Blue Mountain Dam, Cabot 4SW, Evening Shade 1NNE, Mammoth Spring, Mountain View, Sparkman, Alum Fork, and the Clarksville Water Plant.

Isolated severe thunderstorms developed once again across the region during the afternoon of June 15th.  These thunderstorms developed under a very unstable airmass and weak capping in the atmosphere.  Very high lapse rates were present throughout the atmosphere as well which was prone for the storms to produce large hail.  Damaging winds also accompanied the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported in the southeast portion of Texarkana, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level disturbance moved southeast out of the Southern Plains and into the the middle Red River valley of Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas and Northwest Louisiana during the predawn hours of June 12th. These storms propagated into a linear line of severe thunderstorms and proved to be prolific wind makers as numerous trees were downed across the region. Wind damage was widespread...especially across portions of North Central Louisiana where the linear line of storms took on the distinction of a derecho as they accelerated eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed across the entire county.  Trees were downed across County Road 18, Hwy. 237, South State Line Road and Hwy. 71.

Rainfall was widely scattered across northwestern Arkansas early in the month of June 2012 but severely lacking during the latter half of the month. By the end of the month, persistent hot and dry conditions had become established across the region and drought conditions had returned to the area. Much of northwestern and west central Arkansas only received between 10 and 50 percent of normal rainfall for the month, resulting in severe drought conditions (D2) developing across the region. By the end of the month, much of Franklin and Carroll Counties had already slipped into extreme drought (D3). The drought worsened across the region during the month of July. Monetary damage estimates resulting from the drought were not available.

By the end of the month, much of Franklin and Carroll Counties had already slipped into extreme drought (D3). The drought worsened across the region during the month of July. Monetary damage estimates resulting from the drought were not available.

A powerful Mesoscale Convective system developed over Missouri during the morning hours of June 11, 2012. The complex moved southeast into the Mid-South during the afternoon and early evening resulting in numerous reports of damaging winds. There were also a few reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered trees and power lines were knocked down by straight line winds across Phillips County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire, which began as a controlled burn to burn off a wheat field on June 22rd was believed to be extinguished later that day.  Burning embers took off with increased winds and lower humidity and the fire quickly rekindled on June 23rd.  The fire was finally extinguished during the afternoon of Saturday, June 23rd but not before 530 acres of grassland and 7 acres of timberland were destroyed.  Two combines were also destroyed in the fire.

During the last week in June, almost all observing sites rose to at least 100 degrees. The hottest temperature was 111 degrees at the forestry reporting station in Evening Shade. Batesville Lock and Dam reached 110. Readings of 109 were recorded at Mountain Home, Blanchard Springs, Guy, Russellville, and Searcy. Stations that set records for the hottest day ever recorded in the month of June included: Dardanelle, Gilbert, Marshall, Star City/Cane Creek State Park, Batesville Airport, Hot Springs Airport, Harrison, Little Rock, Monticello, Little Rock Air Force Base, North Little Rock, Russellville, Batesville Lock and Dam, Greers Ferry Dam, Blue Mountain Dam, Cabot 4SW, Evening Shade 1NNE, Mammoth Spring, Mountain View, Sparkman, Alum Fork, and the Clarksville Water Plant.

A vigorous upper level disturbance moved southeast out of the Southern Plains and into the the middle Red River valley of Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma, Southwest Arkansas and Northwest Louisiana during the predawn hours of June 12th. These storms propagated into a linear line of severe thunderstorms and proved to be prolific wind makers as numerous trees were downed across the region. Wind damage was widespread...especially across portions of North Central Louisiana where the linear line of storms took on the distinction of a derecho as they accelerated eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed throughout the county.  Trees were downed on South Calhoun and Renfro Roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable airmass developed ahead of a cold front which moved through Southern Arkansas during the afternoon and evening hours of May 7th.  While atmospheric wind shear was relatively weak, the extreme instability made up for the lack of shear.  Multicell and supercell thunderstorms were the end result, some of which produced large hail initially but the storms quickly transitioned into damaging wind producing thunderstorms with numerous reports of wind damage received across Southwest Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported in Umpire, Arkansas.

Stations that set records for the hottest day ever recorded in the month of June included: Dardanelle, Gilbert, Marshall, Star City/Cane Creek State Park, Batesville Airport, Hot Springs Airport, Harrison, Little Rock, Monticello, Little Rock Air Force Base, North Little Rock, Russellville, Batesville Lock and Dam, Greers Ferry Dam, Blue Mountain Dam, Cabot 4SW, Evening Shade 1NNE, Mammoth Spring, Mountain View, Sparkman, Alum Fork, and the Clarksville Water Plant.

A powerful Mesoscale Convective system developed over Missouri during the morning hours of June 11, 2012. The complex moved southeast into the Mid-South during the afternoon and early evening resulting in numerous reports of damaging winds. There were also a few reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight line winds knocked down power poles on County Road 940 just east of Dyess.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very unstable airmass developed ahead of a cold front which moved through Southern Arkansas during the afternoon and evening hours of May 7th.  While atmospheric wind shear was relatively weak, the extreme instability made up for the lack of shear.  Multicell and supercell thunderstorms were the end result, some of which produced large hail initially but the storms quickly transitioned into damaging wind producing thunderstorms with numerous reports of wind damage received across Southwest Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported downed near the Ogden area.  Powerlines were also downed.

An approaching cold front triggered storms in northern and eastern Arkansas on the afternoon and evening of the 20th.
On the 20th, a wildfire 5.7 miles south of Daisy in Pike County burned 132 acres.

Isolated severe thunderstorms developed once again across the region during the afternoon of June 15th.  These thunderstorms developed under a very unstable airmass and weak capping in the atmosphere.  Very high lapse rates were present throughout the atmosphere as well which was prone for the storms to produce large hail.  Damaging winds also accompanied the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in Genoa, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through Arkansas set off a few severe thunderstorms in southern Arkansas during the late afternoon and evening hours on the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Arkansas 83.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm developed and became severe in northern Arkansas on the evening of the 15th.
A cold front moving through Arkansas sparked a few severe thunderstorms on the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck an electric substation, knocking out power to more than 3000 customers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving through Arkansas sparked a few severe thunderstorms on the 17th.

Numerous thunderstorms developed in advance of a strong cold front on the evening of the 19th and continued into the wee hours of the 20th. Some of the storms became severe, producing damaging winds. There was one tornado as well. Just before the rain came to an end, winds brought in dust into Arkansas, causing a mud rain and dirtying thousands of cars. The dust was picked up by a dust storm in West Texas on the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level low pressure system tracked across the Mid-South into the Ohio Valley the night of December 25th, 2012 into the morning of December 26th, 2012. The system brought heavy snow and gusty winds to much of Eastern Arkansas. Blizzard conditions occurred with visibilities of less than a quarter mile and wind gusts of 35 mph or greater for several hours across Lawrence and Randolph Counties. Snow accumulations ranged from three to twelve inches across Eastern Arkansas, mainly northwest of a Wheatley to Blytheville line. The higher snow totals were result of thunder snow which occurred in a few snow bands across Northeast Arkansas. The strong winds knocked down trees and power lines. A few homes were damaged from fallen trees. Roads were hazardous and numerous accidents occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Walnut Ridge AWOS reported blizzard conditions for three hours. Wind gusts were as high as 43 mph with visibilities of less than a quarter mile. Thunder was also reported. Randolph County received the second highest snow totals with amounts between eight and twelve inches with higher amounts in snow drifts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed over eastern Oklahoma during the early evening hours of the 19th as a cold front moved into the region from the west. The thunderstorms moved rapidly eastward into Arkansas, producing damaging wind, large hail, and a tornado across west central Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm wind damaged the awning of a gas station near Zero Street and Wheeler Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed over eastern Oklahoma during the early evening hours of the 19th as a cold front moved into the region from the west. The thunderstorms moved rapidly eastward into Arkansas, producing damaging wind, large hail, and a tornado across west central Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Fort Smith Regional Airport measured thunderstorm wind gusts to 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on Christmas Day, a stationary front was located to the south of Arkansas. During the day, a low pressure center formed along the front, eventually tracking across southeast Arkansas on the evening of the 25th and on toward Middle Tennessee by midnight on the 26th.||During the first part of the 25th, a cold rain fell over most of Arkansas. Then, colder air began spilling into the state to the north and west of the low pressure center's track. Over the northwestern half of Arkansas, a transition from rain to freezing rain, sleet, and some snow occurred during the afternoon, with a changeover to mostly snow by evening. Over the southeastern half of the state, the changeover occurred during the evening, with mostly snow occurring by midnight. Snow began tapering off from the west after midnight, and most of the snow had exited the state by midday on the 26th.||In parts of central and western Arkansas, 0.1 to 0.2 inch of freezing rain accrued before the changeover to snow.||The heaviest band of snow fell from the Ouachita Mountains in west central Arkansas through the central part of the state and then on toward northeastern Arkansas. In this band, snowfall accumulations of 10 to 15 inches were common. The largest snowfall totals reported were 18 inches on Petit Jean Mountain (Conway Co.) and at Perryville (Perry Co.), 17.5 inches at Perry (Perry Co.), and 15 inches at Jessieville (Garland Co.) and Union Valley (Perry Co.). Thunderstorms accompanied the snow in many areas. Commerce and transportation were severely affected. Snowfall totals tapered off both to the northwest and southeast of the heaviest band of snow. Parts of northwest Arkansas received less than 1 inch of snow. Likewise, totals were less than 1 inch over a large part of southeast Arkansas.||Strong winds accompanied the winter storm. Sustained winds of 20 to 30 mph were common, and gusts reached 35 to 45 mph in many areas. Near-blizzard conditions occurred in the northeast part of the County Warning Area.||The combination of ice, snow, and wind caused massive power outages. Branches, limbs, and entire trees fell over onto power lines. In the County Warning Area, there were around 200,000 power outages. About 70% of electric customers in Little Rock lost power. Most of the power outages were repaired within a week; cable TV repairs took several days longer.  ||Trees and limbs also fell onto cars and houses. A 60-year-old man was killed in Benton (Saline Co.), when a very large tree crashed into a house. A crane had to be brought in to lift the tree off the victim. In Little Rock, the Public Works Department estimated it could take until summer to collect all the fallen debris, which amounted to about 22,000 tons.||President Barack Obama declared a federal disaster for Garland, Grant, Hot Spring, Lonoke, Perry, Pulaski, and Saline Counties.||Numerous snowfall records were broken:||New all-time calendar day snowfall records were set at Perry (Perry Co.) when 17.5 inches of snow broke the record of 10 inches set in 1944, Morrilton (Conway Co.) when 12.5 inches of snow broke the record of 9 inches set in 1944, and at Batesville Livestock Experiment Station (Independence Co.) when 11 inches of snow broke the record of 10 inches set in 2010. At Cabot 4SW, the snowfall of 10 inches tied the record of 10 inches set in 1988.||Monthly snowfall records were broken or tied at Perry (Perry Co.), Batesville Livestock Experiment Station (Independence Co.), Morrilton (Conway Co.), Mountain View (Stone Co.), Alum Fork (Saline Co.), Little Rock (Pulaski Co.), North Little Rock (Pulaski Co.), Cabot 4SW (Lonoke Co.), Gravelly 1ESE (Yell Co.), Pine Ridge (Montgomery Co.), Damascus 2NNE (Van Buren Co.), Evening Shade 1NNE (Sharp Co.), Greers Ferry Dam (Cleburne Co.), Little Rock Air Force Base (Pulaski Co.), Mena (Polk Co.), Parks (Scott Co.), Crystal Valley (Pulaski Co.), Langley (Pike Co.), Batesville Lock and Dam (Independence Co.), Big Fork 1SSE (Polk Co.), Glenwood (Pike Co.), Bonnerdale 4SSW (Hot Spring Co.), Ratcliff (Logan Co.), Augusta 2NW (Woodruff Co.), Clinton 6SE (Van Buren Co.), and Keo (Lonoke Co.).||The 10.3 inches of snow that fell at Little Rock made it the snowiest Christmas on record. It also made it one of the top ten calendar snowfalls ever recorded, as well as one of the top ten 24-hour snowfall amounts ever recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer at Harrison measured only 0.3 inch of snow, while no snow fell at Omaha.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on Christmas Day, a stationary front was located to the south of Arkansas. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front moved through the Mid-South and clashed with a moist and unstable airmass during the afternoon and evening of December 9, 2012.  A line of showers and thunderstorms developed in advance of the front and produced isolated damaging winds and large hail across parts of Northeast Arkansas.
A cold front moving through Arkansas sparked a few severe thunderstorms on the 17th.

The approach of a cold front triggered several severe thunderstorms in northern and central Arkansas during the afternoon and evening of the 16th. The atmosphere over Arkansas was warm, humid, and unstable at the time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Docks were damaged and trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Grange Fire was started by lightning 5 miles east of Cave City in Sharp County on the 15th. Altogether, 232 acres burned.

Numerous thunderstorms developed in advance of a strong cold front on the evening of the 19th and continued into the wee hours of the 20th. Some of the storms became severe, producing damaging winds. There was one tornado as well. Just before the rain came to an end, winds brought in dust into Arkansas, causing a mud rain and dirtying thousands of cars. The dust was picked up by a dust storm in West Texas on the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down at several locations between Casscoe and Preston Ferry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front moved through the Mid-South and clashed with a moist and unstable airmass during the afternoon and evening of December 9, 2012.  A line of showers and thunderstorms developed in advance of the front and produced isolated damaging winds and large hail across parts of Northeast Arkansas.

An arctic front moved through the Mid-South and clashed with a moist and unstable airmass during the afternoon and evening of December 9, 2012.  A line of showers and thunderstorms developed in advance of the front and produced isolated damaging winds and large hail across parts of Northeast Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging straight line winds snapped a few cedar trees at the ground. A small metal hay barn had roof damage and a partially built home received roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Raspberry Mountain Fire began in the Ouachita National Forest about 7.6 miles south-southwest of Big Fork in Polk County soon after midnight on November 26th. It was controlled on December 17th. Altogether, 873 acres burned.

A cold front, accompanied by numerous thunderstorms, moved through Arkansas on the 4th. The storms brought very frequent lightning, with more than 11,000 cloud-to-ground lightning strikes recorded in the state, along with some locally heavy rain, some street flooding, and small hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a pine tree, then traveled to the ground, where the electrical charge forked. One fork went to a workshop, knocking out the power and melting the breakers in the breaker box. The other fork traveled to a downspout on a house and then up along the gutter on the front of the house. Beams holding up the porch roof were splintered but remained enough intact to support the roof. The lightning then traveled to the flashing on the roof, subsequently setting fire to the roof vents and the roof. Water ran into the house, ruining expensive wood flooring and damaging some furnishings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed in advance of a strong cold front on the evening of the 19th and continued into the wee hours of the 20th. Some of the storms became severe, producing damaging winds. There was one tornado as well. Just before the rain came to an end, winds brought in dust into Arkansas, causing a mud rain and dirtying thousands of cars. The dust was picked up by a dust storm in West Texas on the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter estimated wind gusts at 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A double-wide mobile home was destroyed; the owner suffered minor injuries. The roof of a house was badly damaged. A couple of additional houses had minor roof damage, and one had a couple of windows blown out. Several shop buildings were damaged or destroyed. A camper was overturned. Trees and power lines were blown down. One of the trees fell on a carport, crushing a pickup truck and a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on Christmas Day, a stationary front was located to the south of Arkansas. During the day, a low pressure center formed along the front, eventually tracking across southeast Arkansas on the evening of the 25th and on toward Middle Tennessee by midnight on the 26th.||During the first part of the 25th, a cold rain fell over most of Arkansas. Then, colder air began spilling into the state to the north and west of the low pressure center's track. Over the northwestern half of Arkansas, a transition from rain to freezing rain, sleet, and some snow occurred during the afternoon, with a changeover to mostly snow by evening. Over the southeastern half of the state, the changeover occurred during the evening, with mostly snow occurring by midnight. Snow began tapering off from the west after midnight, and most of the snow had exited the state by midday on the 26th.||In parts of central and western Arkansas, 0.1 to 0.2 inch of freezing rain accrued before the changeover to snow.||The heaviest band of snow fell from the Ouachita Mountains in west central Arkansas through the central part of the state and then on toward northeastern Arkansas. In this band, snowfall accumulations of 10 to 15 inches were common. The largest snowfall totals reported were 18 inches on Petit Jean Mountain (Conway Co.) and at Perryville (Perry Co.), 17.5 inches at Perry (Perry Co.), and 15 inches at Jessieville (Garland Co.) and Union Valley (Perry Co.). Thunderstorms accompanied the snow in many areas. Commerce and transportation were severely affected. Snowfall totals tapered off both to the northwest and southeast of the heaviest band of snow. Parts of northwest Arkansas received less than 1 inch of snow. Likewise, totals were less than 1 inch over a large part of southeast Arkansas.||Strong winds accompanied the winter storm. Sustained winds of 20 to 30 mph were common, and gusts reached 35 to 45 mph in many areas. Near-blizzard conditions occurred in the northeast part of the County Warning Area.||The combination of ice, snow, and wind caused massive power outages. Branches, limbs, and entire trees fell over onto power lines. In the County Warning Area, there were around 200,000 power outages. About 70% of electric customers in Little Rock lost power. Most of the power outages were repaired within a week; cable TV repairs took several days longer.  ||Trees and limbs also fell onto cars and houses. A 60-year-old man was killed in Benton (Saline Co.), when a very large tree crashed into a house. A crane had to be brought in to lift the tree off the victim. In Little Rock, the Public Works Department estimated it could take until summer to collect all the fallen debris, which amounted to about 22,000 tons.||President Barack Obama declared a federal disaster for Garland, Grant, Hot Spring, Lonoke, Perry, Pulaski, and Saline Counties.||Numerous snowfall records were broken:||New all-time calendar day snowfall records were set at Perry (Perry Co.) when 17.5 inches of snow broke the record of 10 inches set in 1944, Morrilton (Conway Co.) when 12.5 inches of snow broke the record of 9 inches set in 1944, and at Batesville Livestock Experiment Station (Independence Co.) when 11 inches of snow broke the record of 10 inches set in 2010. At Cabot 4SW, the snowfall of 10 inches tied the record of 10 inches set in 1988.||Monthly snowfall records were broken or tied at Perry (Perry Co.), Batesville Livestock Experiment Station (Independence Co.), Morrilton (Conway Co.), Mountain View (Stone Co.), Alum Fork (Saline Co.), Little Rock (Pulaski Co.), North Little Rock (Pulaski Co.), Cabot 4SW (Lonoke Co.), Gravelly 1ESE (Yell Co.), Pine Ridge (Montgomery Co.), Damascus 2NNE (Van Buren Co.), Evening Shade 1NNE (Sharp Co.), Greers Ferry Dam (Cleburne Co.), Little Rock Air Force Base (Pulaski Co.), Mena (Polk Co.), Parks (Scott Co.), Crystal Valley (Pulaski Co.), Langley (Pike Co.), Batesville Lock and Dam (Independence Co.), Big Fork 1SSE (Polk Co.), Glenwood (Pike Co.), Bonnerdale 4SSW (Hot Spring Co.), Ratcliff (Logan Co.), Augusta 2NW (Woodruff Co.), Clinton 6SE (Van Buren Co.), and Keo (Lonoke Co.).||The 10.3 inches of snow that fell at Little Rock made it the snowiest Christmas on record. It also made it one of the top ten calendar snowfalls ever recorded, as well as one of the top ten 24-hour snowfall amounts ever recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports in Lonoke County included:  12 inches at Austin (Air Force weather forecaster), 10 inches at Cabot (cooperative observer), 8 inches at Lonoke (public report), 7 inches at Cabot (HAM radio and meteorologist reports), and 5 inches at Keo (cooperative observer).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level low pressure system tracked across the Mid-South into the Ohio Valley the night of December 25th, 2012 into the morning of December 26th, 2012. The system brought heavy snow and gusty winds to much of Eastern Arkansas. Blizzard conditions occurred with visibilities of less than a quarter mile and wind gusts of 35 mph or greater for several hours across Lawrence and Randolph Counties. Snow accumulations ranged from three to twelve inches across Eastern Arkansas, mainly northwest of a Wheatley to Blytheville line. The higher snow totals were result of thunder snow which occurred in a few snow bands across Northeast Arkansas. The strong winds knocked down trees and power lines. A few homes were damaged from fallen trees. Roads were hazardous and numerous accidents occurred as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations ranged from four to seven inches across Craighead County. A tree fell onto a house producing damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions changed very little in December, with extreme drought lingering in much of western Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of December, severe drought covered the extreme northern part of Baxter County, with extreme drought elsewhere. Conditions changed little during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions changed very little in December, with extreme drought lingering in much of western Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of December, extreme drought covered all of Newton County, except the southwestern corner of the county, where severe drought prevailed. Conditions changed little during the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions changed very little in December, with extreme drought lingering in much of western Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of December, severe drought covered a small portion of western Independence County. This area shrank slightly during the month. Elsewhere, only moderate drought was noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed in advance of a strong cold front on the evening of the 19th and continued into the wee hours of the 20th. Some of the storms became severe, producing damaging winds. There was one tornado as well. Just before the rain came to an end, winds brought in dust into Arkansas, causing a mud rain and dirtying thousands of cars. The dust was picked up by a dust storm in West Texas on the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Sheridan, part of the roof was blown off at a consignment shop, the metal roof was blown off the Masonic Lodge, the roof was blown off an oil company, a carport and a garage collapsed, shingles were blown off many roofs, and trees and power lines were blown down. East of Sheridan, trees were blown down at a number of locations along U.S. 270.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of heavy rainfall events in mid to late December and a heavy snowstorm on Christmas Day, caused the Cache River to have minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple heavy rain events throughout the month of December dropped 5 to 6 inches of rain. In addition, a heavy snowstorm on Christmas Day dropped 6 inches of snowfall locally, with between 8 and 12 inches of snow upstream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very tropical airmass, weak surface boundaries and disturbances aloft resulted in very sufficient rain producers across Southwest Arkansas during the afternoon of July 9th.  Some flash flooding was reported in Columbia County, Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some cars were flooded and water was beginning to enter a few businesses in downtown Magnolia, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms, accompanying a cold front, moved across Arkansas on the afternoon and the evening of the 11th. A lightning strike caused damage to a church in Little Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a church near downtown Little Rock. A chimney and a corner of the bell tower were knocked apart. The falling stones and bricks punched several holes in the tile roof, and rafters were split or shattered in several places. Heavy rain caused water to pour into the attic and work its way down a couple of floors into the choir loft of the sanctuary. The church's phone lines and elevator were knocked out. The church, built in 1921, is on the National Registry of Historic Places.

At the end of December, severe drought covered only the extreme northwest part of the county, with moderate drought over northern and central parts of the county, and only abnormally dry conditions in the south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As temperatures soared into the triple digits during the afternoon of the 28th, isolated showers and thunderstorms developed over South Central Arkansas and North Central Louisiana, along a weak backdoor surface front that drifted southwest into the region. The very hot air mass resulted in moderate instability developing over this area, with additional showers and thunderstorms rapidly developing along resultant outflow boundaries while propagating west southwest across the area. Several severe thunderstorm clusters resulted, with numerous reports of downed trees and power lines across El Dorado and southern Union County Arkansas/northwest Union Parish Louisiana, as well as portions of North Central Louisiana to the south. These storms diminished during the late evening hours with the loss of heating/instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed between El Dorado and Junction City, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold air mass was gradually retreating from Arkansas on the 13th, but precipitation moved in from the west before the cold air could exit. In most parts of Arkansas, precipitation began as snow. Then, as warmer air moved in during the day, the snow changed to a mixture of sleet and freezing rain. This occurred during the morning in southern and central sections, and during the afternoon in the north. Finally, the wintry mix changed over to rain as warmer air continued to invade the state. This changeover occurred during the morning in the south, around midday in central sections, and during the afternoon or early evening in the north. Snowfall totals over the southern half of the state were well less than an inch. Over the northern half, most snow accumulations were in the 1 to 3 inch range. However, totals to 4 inches were reported in the higher elevations of Newton County south of Jasper, and in northern Baxter County. Freezing rain amounts ranged from a trace to around .10 inch.

The Mt. Riante Fire began on July 29th southwest of Lake Hamilton in Garland County. Several fire departments, the Arkansas Forestry Commission, and the U.S. Forest Service fought the fire. In addition, the Arkansas National Guard used Black Hawk helicopters to drop water. Fire engines were stationed at homes in the area. The fire was finally controlled on August 3rd, after burning 355 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Gurdon 1 Fire near Gurdon in Clark County burned 160 acres on the 29th. Four fire departments and the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought the fire.

The Springlake Road Fire 4 miles east of East End in Saline County burned 100 acres on the 30th. Sixty people were evacuated at the height of the fire, but the fire was stopped before any structures burned. Five fire departments and the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought the fire. The fire was ruled to be arson.

The Clinton Fire began near Cleveland in Conway County and eventually spread into Van Buren County. The fire burned 110 acres. Three firefighters were treated for heat exhaustion. At least five fire departments and the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought the fire.

Thunderstorms developed to the north of Arkansas early on the 11th. The storms moved into northern Arkansas around midday and crossed much of the northern part of the state early in the afternoon. Later in the afternoon, the storms crossed through central Arkansas, and by early evening, the storms had moved through the southeast part of the state. Many of the storms produced wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph. This resulted in areas of blowing dust in central and eastern Arkansas. Some of the storms became severe, producing damaging winds and hail. At mid to late evening, scattered storms redeveloped over central Arkansas and a large area of showers and thunderstorms moved into western Arkansas from Oklahoma. Through the overnight hours and into the morning of the 12th, numerous showers and thunderstorms affected the southern half of Arkansas. A few storms produced damaging winds and very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The fire department estimated winds at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High instability, a warm front, and an upper level low pressure system came together to create severe storms across northern and central Arkansas on the 3rd and 4th.

The high pressure aloft that had been over Arkansas since June finally drifted to the west. This allowed some tropical moisture to return to the state. Scattered thunderstorms developed on the 6th, 7th, and 8th, and some of these storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs were thrown into the air, and some of the limbs landed on vehicles.

Thunderstorms developed to the north of Arkansas early on the 11th. The storms moved into northern Arkansas around midday and crossed much of the northern part of the state early in the afternoon. Later in the afternoon, the storms crossed through central Arkansas, and by early evening, the storms had moved through the southeast part of the state. Many of the storms produced wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph. This resulted in areas of blowing dust in central and eastern Arkansas. Some of the storms became severe, producing damaging winds and hail. At mid to late evening, scattered storms redeveloped over central Arkansas and a large area of showers and thunderstorms moved into western Arkansas from Oklahoma. Through the overnight hours and into the morning of the 12th, numerous showers and thunderstorms affected the southern half of Arkansas. A few storms produced damaging winds and very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was snapped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot temperatures occurred on the 29th, with many areas reaching the mid to upper 90s. The hot weather helped spark an isolated thunderstorm in Saline County during the afternoon. This storm tracked into Hot Spring County, bringing numerous reports of hail and a few of wind damage. The storm weakened over Dallas County early in the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stagnant area of high pressure aloft was centered across the central and northern Plains. As a result, flow around this area high pressure was from the east to the west across the Four State Region. Weak disturbances moved into the region from Mississippi and this combined with daytime heating, a tropical like airmass and moderate instability, resulted in the development of scattered strong to severe thunderstorms. These thunderstorms were pulse type storms and produced strong and damaging wind gusts as they collapsed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines were downed in El Dorado, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Amity Fire occurred on the 4th 6.2 miles northeast of Amity in Clark County and then spread into Hot Spring County. Altogether, 350 acres burned. A number of fire departments, the Arkansas Forestry Commission, and the U.S. Forest Service fought the fire, which was started by a hay baling machine. Six farm buildings, the hay baler, and a tractor were destroyed.

The Amity Fire occurred on the 4th 6.2 miles northeast of Amity in Clark County and then spread into Hot Spring County. Altogether, 350 acres burned. A number of fire departments, the Arkansas Forestry Commission, and the U.S. Forest Service fought the fire, which was started by a hay baling machine. Six farm buildings, the hay baler, and a tractor were destroyed.

A wildfire in the Mountain Springs area of northwest Lonoke County burned 4 acres on the 4th. A mobile home, two barns, and several vehicles were destroyed. Investigators indicated that the use of fireworks caused the fire. Lonoke County was under a burn ban at the time which prohibited the use of fireworks. Three people were arrested and charged with felony criminal mischief.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Hog Thief Fire 2.4 miles southeast of Magazine in Logan County began on the 1st and was controlled on the 3rd. Altogether, 293 acres burned. Fifteen homes were evacuated as a precaution but the homes were not damaged. A number of fire departments, the Arkansas Forestry Commission, and the U.S. Forest Service fought the fire.

The Leola Fire about 5 miles southwest of Leola in Dallas County burned 200 acres on the 4th. Six fire departments, the Arkansas Forestry Commission, the Grant County Sheriff's Office, Dallas County Sheriff's Office, and Dallas County Office of Emergency Management all responded to the fire. A National Guard helicopter was used to drop water on the fire.

The Dallas 330 Fire about 6 miles southwest of Leola in Dallas County burned 115 acres on the 4th. Six fire departments, the Arkansas Forestry Commission, the Grant County Sheriff's Office, Dallas County Sheriff's Office, and Dallas County Office of Emergency Management all responded to the fire.  A National Guard helicopter was used to drop water on the fire.

The month of July continued where June left off concerning the increasing drought conditions across all of Southwest Arkansas.  While portions of Southwest Arkansas did see rainfall amounts that were above normal for the month, the higher totals were very isolated. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon of May 21st ahead of a weak cold front moving across the Deep South. Wind damage, large hail and some flash flooding all occurred as the activity moved across the region.

During the late afternoon and evening on April 3, a cold front pushed into the region. A warm and moist environment was in place ahead of the front across west-central Arkansas. Strong to severe thunderstorms developed over the area, resulting in large hail, damaging winds and some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm wind blew down numerous power poles and power lines.

Early morning thunderstorms occurred over far northwest Arkansas in advance of a slow moving cold front. Damaging winds occurred from these storms in Washington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm wind blew down large trees and snapped large tree limbs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm wind blew down large tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Mt. Riante Fire began on the 29th southwest of Lake Hamilton in Garland County. Several fire departments, the Arkansas Forestry Commission, and the U.S. Forest Service fought the fire. In addition, the Arkansas National Guard used Black Hawk helicopters to drop water. Fire engines were stationed at homes in the area. The fire continued into August.

The high pressure aloft that had been over Arkansas since June finally drifted to the west. This allowed some tropical moisture to return to the state. Scattered thunderstorms developed on the 6th, 7th, and 8th, and some of these storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a business in Batesville. The air conditioning, phone circuits, and circuit breakers were destroyed. A fire started in the front of the building, badly damaging it. The company sold cylinders of industrial gases, but the fire did not reach any of the cylinders.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Ola Fire began on the 25th 3 miles southeast of Ola in Yell County. The fire was contained on the 28th after destroying 1014 acres, a mobile home, a barn, several vehicles, and a major power transmission line. This was the largest wildfire so far this year in Arkansas. At the height of the fire, the town of Ola (population about 1300) was evacuated, and portions of Arkansas 7 and Arkansas 10 were closed. A fireworks warehouse was threatened, but the fire did not reach the business. Numerous fire departments, the Arkansas Forestry Commission, the U.S. Forest Service, the Yell County Road Department, Deltic Timber Co., and Terra Renewal Services fought the fire.

Unusually dry weather occurred in Arkansas during May. The following stations set a record for the driest May on record:  Clinton 6SE, Morrilton, Dardanelle, Arkadelphia 2N, Gilbert, Damascus 2NNE, Marshall, Hot Springs Airport, Perry, Hot Springs National Park, Danville, Nimrod Dam, Little Rock Air Force Base, Clarksville Water Plant, Sparkman, North Little Rock, Harrison, Blakely Mountain Dam, Jasper, Mountain View, Murfreesboro 1W, Rohwer 2NNE, Blue Mountain Dam, Alum Fork, Clarksville 6NE, Batesville Airport, Subiaco, Sheridan, Arkansas Post, Amity 1N, and Ozone. Although some rain occurred during the first half of June, rainfall was extremely sparse during the latter half of the month. The general lack of rainfall led to drought conditions and steadily rising temperatures. Severe drought developed over most of Arkansas soon after the middle of June. By the end of the month, extreme drought covered parts of northern Arkansas, the Arkansas River Valley, and parts of southwest and south central Arkansas. The following stations set a record for the driest June on record: Salem, Damascus 2NNE, Blakely Mountain Dam, Beedeville 4NE, Clarksville 6NE, and the Clarksville Water Plant. During the last week in June, almost all observing sites rose to at least 100 degrees. The hottest temperature was 111 degrees at the forestry reporting station in Evening Shade. Batesville Lock and Dam reached 110. Readings of 109 were recorded at Mountain Home, Blanchard Springs, Guy, Russellville, and Searcy. Stations that set records for the hottest day ever recorded in the month of June included: Dardanelle, Gilbert, Marshall, Star City/Cane Creek State Park, Batesville Airport, Hot Springs Airport, Harrison, Little Rock, Monticello, Little Rock Air Force Base, North Little Rock, Russellville, Batesville Lock and Dam, Greers Ferry Dam, Blue Mountain Dam, Cabot 4SW, Evening Shade 1NNE, Mammoth Spring, Mountain View, Sparkman, Alum Fork, and the Clarksville Water Plant. As a result of the hot, dry weather, almost every county in Arkansas was placed under a burn ban. Fireworks sales leading up to Independence Day were very poor, as many cities and counties prohibited the use of fireworks. The Arkansas Forestry Commission raised the state's fire danger to Extreme, a category which is used very rarely. Hundreds of wildfires occurred, but the vast majority of these burned less than 100 acres. Pastures dried up, causing many cattle growers to send their livestock to market. Some stock ponds dried up, as well. Several counties declared emergencies so that volunteer fire departments could haul water to stock ponds on private property. Some water utilities instituted mandatory water restrictions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed over Boone County soon after the middle of June. Before the end of the month, the drought worsened to extreme.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a strong upper level disturbance, rich gulf moisture, and good instability helped to develop a strong squall line that moved north out of southeast Louisiana causing scattered hail and thunderstorm wind damage across portions of southeast Arkansas. The thunderstorm wind damage was mainly in the form of blown down power lines and trees as well as some spotty damage to structures. One tree fell on a mobile home, which resulted in an injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down around Crossett. One tree was blown down onto a mobile home injuring one occupant inside. A few power lines were also blown down around town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a cold front moving through the region. A few of the storms produced damaging wind and large hail as they moved across northwest Arkansas.

Unusually dry weather occurred in Arkansas during May. The following stations set a record for the driest May on record:  Clinton 6SE, Morrilton, Dardanelle, Arkadelphia 2N, Gilbert, Damascus 2NNE, Marshall, Hot Springs Airport, Perry, Hot Springs National Park, Danville, Nimrod Dam, Little Rock Air Force Base, Clarksville Water Plant, Sparkman, North Little Rock, Harrison, Blakely Mountain Dam, Jasper, Mountain View, Murfreesboro 1W, Rohwer 2NNE, Blue Mountain Dam, Alum Fork, Clarksville 6NE, Batesville Airport, Subiaco, Sheridan, Arkansas Post, Amity 1N, and Ozone. Although some rain occurred during the first half of June, rainfall was extremely sparse during the latter half of the month. The general lack of rainfall led to drought conditions and steadily rising temperatures. Severe drought developed over most of Arkansas soon after the middle of June. By the end of the month, extreme drought covered parts of northern Arkansas, the Arkansas River Valley, and parts of southwest and south central Arkansas. The following stations set a record for the driest June on record: Salem, Damascus 2NNE, Blakely Mountain Dam, Beedeville 4NE, Clarksville 6NE, and the Clarksville Water Plant. During the last week in June, almost all observing sites rose to at least 100 degrees. The hottest temperature was 111 degrees at the forestry reporting station in Evening Shade. Batesville Lock and Dam reached 110. Readings of 109 were recorded at Mountain Home, Blanchard Springs, Guy, Russellville, and Searcy. Stations that set records for the hottest day ever recorded in the month of June included: Dardanelle, Gilbert, Marshall, Star City/Cane Creek State Park, Batesville Airport, Hot Springs Airport, Harrison, Little Rock, Monticello, Little Rock Air Force Base, North Little Rock, Russellville, Batesville Lock and Dam, Greers Ferry Dam, Blue Mountain Dam, Cabot 4SW, Evening Shade 1NNE, Mammoth Spring, Mountain View, Sparkman, Alum Fork, and the Clarksville Water Plant. As a result of the hot, dry weather, almost every county in Arkansas was placed under a burn ban. Fireworks sales leading up to Independence Day were very poor, as many cities and counties prohibited the use of fireworks. The Arkansas Forestry Commission raised the state's fire danger to Extreme, a category which is used very rarely. Hundreds of wildfires occurred, but the vast majority of these burned less than 100 acres. Pastures dried up, causing many cattle growers to send their livestock to market. Some stock ponds dried up, as well. Several counties declared emergencies so that volunteer fire departments could haul water to stock ponds on private property. Some water utilities instituted mandatory water restrictions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed over Scott County soon after the middle of June and then continued through the end of the month.

A Mesoscale Convective Vortex (low pressure left behind by previous thunderstorms) traveled across central and southern Arkansas, causing several severe thunderstorms on the afternoon of the 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 1.15 inches of rain fell at the National Weather Service office at the North Little Rock airport between 3:00 PM and 3:55 PM CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed to the north of Arkansas early on the 11th. The storms moved into northern Arkansas around midday and crossed much of the northern part of the state early in the afternoon. Later in the afternoon, the storms crossed through central Arkansas, and by early evening, the storms had moved through the southeast part of the state. Many of the storms produced wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph. This resulted in areas of blowing dust in central and eastern Arkansas. Some of the storms became severe, producing damaging winds and hail. At mid to late evening, scattered storms redeveloped over central Arkansas and a large area of showers and thunderstorms moved into western Arkansas from Oklahoma. Through the overnight hours and into the morning of the 12th, numerous showers and thunderstorms affected the southern half of Arkansas. A few storms produced damaging winds and very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A shop building was destroyed and debris from the shop punctured a grain bin. Pieces of metal from the shop flew into a corn crop, shearing off plants. Two barns were damaged by flying debris. A fuel tank and spray rig were overturned. A tractor was damaged. Much of the metal roof was torn off a shed. Trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Shelterwood 149 Fire burned 685 acres in the Ouachita National Forest. This fire was located in Montgomery County, in the Government Hill area about 2.75 miles north- northeast of Sims. The origin of the fire was determined to be lightning. No structures were lost.

An attempt to burn a pile of bamboo led to a 5 acre wildfire at East End in Saline County on the 28th. Seven fire departments fought the blaze. Two firefighters suffered heat exhaustion. Because a burn ban was in effect at the time of the fire, the landowner was issued a citation with a fine of $1100.

Stations that set records for the hottest day ever recorded in the month of June included: Dardanelle, Gilbert, Marshall, Star City/Cane Creek State Park, Batesville Airport, Hot Springs Airport, Harrison, Little Rock, Monticello, Little Rock Air Force Base, North Little Rock, Russellville, Batesville Lock and Dam, Greers Ferry Dam, Blue Mountain Dam, Cabot 4SW, Evening Shade 1NNE, Mammoth Spring, Mountain View, Sparkman, Alum Fork, and the Clarksville Water Plant. As a result of the hot, dry weather, almost every county in Arkansas was placed under a burn ban. Fireworks sales leading up to Independence Day were very poor, as many cities and counties prohibited the use of fireworks. The Arkansas Forestry Commission raised the state's fire danger to Extreme, a category which is used very rarely. Hundreds of wildfires occurred, but the vast majority of these burned less than 100 acres. Pastures dried up, causing many cattle growers to send their livestock to market. Some stock ponds dried up, as well. Several counties declared emergencies so that volunteer fire departments could haul water to stock ponds on private property. Some water utilities instituted mandatory water restrictions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought developed over the southwestern corner of Drew County soon after the middle of June and then continued through the end of the month.

Unusually dry weather occurred in Arkansas during May. The following stations set a record for the driest May on record:  Clinton 6SE, Morrilton, Dardanelle, Arkadelphia 2N, Gilbert, Damascus 2NNE, Marshall, Hot Springs Airport, Perry, Hot Springs National Park, Danville, Nimrod Dam, Little Rock Air Force Base, Clarksville Water Plant, Sparkman, North Little Rock, Harrison, Blakely Mountain Dam, Jasper, Mountain View, Murfreesboro 1W, Rohwer 2NNE, Blue Mountain Dam, Alum Fork, Clarksville 6NE, Batesville Airport, Subiaco, Sheridan, Arkansas Post, Amity 1N, and Ozone. Although some rain occurred during the first half of June, rainfall was extremely sparse during the latter half of the month. The general lack of rainfall led to drought conditions and steadily rising temperatures. Severe drought developed over most of Arkansas soon after the middle of June. By the end of the month, extreme drought covered parts of northern Arkansas, the Arkansas River Valley, and parts of southwest and south central Arkansas. Some water utilities instituted mandatory water restrictions.

The Turkey Pen Hollow Fire 4.2 miles east of Pleasant Plains in Independence County burned 199 acres on the 22nd.

A warm front pushed north across the region and eventually stalled across northern sections of the Mid-South by the late afternoon on June 3, 2012.  An upper level disturbance moving through triggered numerous showers and thunderstorms along the warm front and eventually formed a mesoscale convective system that pushed through during the evening hours of June 3, 2012.  There were reports of damaging winds and large hail.  After midnight additional showers and thunderstorms redeveloped along the stalled front producing a few more reports of damaging winds during the early morning hours of June 4, 2012.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damaging straight line winds knocked down trees across County Road 766 at Highway 141.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An additional cluster of showers and thunderstorms moved across the delta portion of Mississippi and Arkansas in wake of a derecho that moved across the region earlier in the evening. These storms caused some flooding in Dermott.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of streets in town were under water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Below normal rainfall fell during the month of June across Eastern Arkansas. As a result, severe (D2) drought conditions spread across all of Eastern Arkansas by the end of June. In addition, drought conditions worsened to extreme (D3) conditions across Northeast Arkansas during the last week of June. Many crops that were planted during the spring struggled to grow due to lack of water. Irreversible damage was done to the corn crop while soybean and sorghum fields yield potential continued to dwindle as a result of the dry conditions. Many pastures were in poor condition forcing farmers to feed cattle baled hay. Lake and river levels dropped to low levels. Burn bans were issued for many counties as a result of the dry conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions began across the county.
