At the beginning of August, most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought. After a brief heat wave at the beginning of the month, the weather returned closer to a normal weather pattern, with weather systems bringing at least scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time. By the end of the month, there had been slight improvement in the drought in parts of western and central Arkansas. During the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought 425 fires, which burned 7721 acres. There were hundreds of additional, smaller fires fought by city fire departments and rural volunteer departments. The passage of Tropical Depression Isaac at the end of the month brought more substantial rainfall and consequent improvement in the drought. The drought improvement was reflected in the first Drought Monitor issued in September and will thus appear in September's Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional drought covered Van Buren County throughout the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure, which had been nearly stationary over Arkansas, shifted to the west, allowing areas of low pressure aloft to move southeastward into Arkansas periodically. Scattered thunderstorms developed with each of the low pressure areas and isolated storms became severe on the 1st and 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were blown down, with one of the trees landing on a house.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tin roofing was removed from several houses. Trees and power lines were blown down. Some power poles were snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought as August began. Unusual heat also continued into the beginning of the month. From the 1st through the 5th, high temperatures were 100 degrees or higher at many reporting stations. The highest temperatures during this time frame were 108 at Cabot and Silver Hill; 107 at Russellville, Conway, Booneville forestry station, and Oden; 106 at Little Rock, DeGray Lake State Park, Dardanelle, Subiaco, Morrilton, Leola, Malvern, Nimrod Dam, and Jessieville. After the first few days of the month, the high pressure aloft that had caused hot weather throughout July shifted to the west. This allowed at least some scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time, and heat indices gradually dropped below 105 in most areas. On the 3rd, a 16 month-old girl died in a hot car in Newport (Jackson Co.). According to police reports, the father was supposed to drop the baby off at daycare but forgot to do so. Thus, the baby remained in the car all day and was not discovered in the car until late afternoon. The high temperature at Newport that day reached 96 degrees and the heat index reached 106. The father was arrested at the scene and charged with negligent homicide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cluster of thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front during the wee hours of the 13th. The storms had their biggest effect down the Arkansas River Valley. Impressive rainfall amounts occurred in parts of central Arkansas, especially considering the area was in an exceptional drought. Additional storms developed during the late afternoon and evening along the cold front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although Isaac was a hurricane in the Gulf of Mexico, it had weakened to a tropical depression by the time it reached Arkansas. Isaac passed through western Arkansas on the evening of the 30th and during the day on the 31st. Positions from the National Hurricane Center and the Hydrometeorological Prediction Center:  August 30th at 10 PM (CDT) 95 miles south-southwest of Little Rock, August 31st at 4 AM 95 miles west of Little Rock, August 31st at 10 AM 25 miles northeast of Fort Smith, and August 31st at 4 PM 40 miles east of Joplin, Missouri. Although Isaac had exited Arkansas by the end of the month, some effects, including bands of thunderstorms spiraling in toward its center, continued through September 1st into the wee hours of the 2nd. Isaac brought sustained winds of 23 to 33 mph and wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph in many parts of the state. The highest sustained wind in the Little Rock County Warning Area was 33 mph at Little Rock Air Force Base, and the strongest gust was 46 mph at the Pine Bluff airport. The strong winds over a prolonged period caused large numbers of trees to fall. Some of these trees had been weakened by the ongoing drought. Falling limbs and trees took down power lines, leaving more than 20,000 electric customers in the dark. Isaac also brought widespread rain. Three injuries were reported. Two people were injured in Little Rock when a tree fell on a car parked in a driveway; one person was injured in White Hall (Jefferson Co.) when a tree fell on a car. Where bands of showers and thunderstorms lingered over a particular area or where rain moved over the same area repeatedly, flash flooding occurred. Some of the larger storm totals included 11.29 inches at White Hall (Jefferson Co.), 10.70 inches at Griffithville (White Co.), 10.65 inches at the Pine Bluff Water Plant (Jefferson Co.), 8.90 inches at the Pine Bluff airport, 8.88 inches at Judsonia (White Co.), 8.40 inches at Steprock (White Co.), and 8.11 inches at Fordyce (Dallas Co.). The lowest barometric pressure reported in the Little Rock County Warning area was 29.51 inches at Camden. The combination of wind and rain caused damage in the rice crop, due to lodging of the rice. Cotton, corn, and soybeans appeared to have only minor damage. The rain improved prospects for the hay crop, and pastures providing forage for livestock improved.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall measured 4.08 inches in 24 hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall measured 4.02 inches in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of August, most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought. After a brief heat wave at the beginning of the month, the weather returned closer to a normal weather pattern, with weather systems bringing at least scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time. By the end of the month, there had been slight improvement in the drought in parts of western and central Arkansas. During the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought 425 fires, which burned 7721 acres. There were hundreds of additional, smaller fires fought by city fire departments and rural volunteer departments. The passage of Tropical Depression Isaac at the end of the month brought more substantial rainfall and consequent improvement in the drought. The drought improvement was reflected in the first Drought Monitor issued in September and will thus appear in September's Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Throughout the month, exceptional drought covered the northwestern half of Prairie County and extreme drought covered the southeastern half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually hot weather that began in late June continued into the first part of July, thanks to a large area of high pressure aloft. Many high temperatures of 100 degrees or higher were recorded, and a number of daily high temperature records were broken. Although enough moisture returned such that very isolated showers and thunderstorms occurred each day, the rain did little to help the drought. Early in the month, about a third of Arkansas was affected by extreme drought, with severe drought existing in almost all other parts of the state. Pastures dried up, causing many cattle growers to send their livestock to market. Some stock ponds dried up, as well. Some water utilities instituted mandatory water restrictions. The hot, dry weather contributed to the spread of wildfires. At the beginning of the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission rated fire danger statewide as Extreme, a category which is used very rarely. County judges instituted burn bans for virtually every county in Arkansas. Sales of fireworks for the 4th of July were greatly affected, with some fireworks stands closing before the holiday. Some municipal fireworks shows were cancelled as a result of the dry weather. Dozens of fires broke out each day, but only a relatively small number of these exceeded 100 acres. On the 3rd, Arkansas Governor Mike Beebe authorized the National Guard to use Black Hawk helicopters to assist firefighters by dropping water on the larger fires using 660 gallon Bambi Buckets. On the 4th alone, the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought 43 wildfires which burned 1803 acres. During the first half of the month, many rivers in the state fell to unusually low levels. Twenty stations recorded levels that were in their top ten lowest water levels since the last big agricultural drought in 2000. The U.S. Secretary of Agriculture designated almost all of Arkansas as being in a drought disaster. Tropical moisture began returning to Arkansas about the 6th of July. This brought higher humidities, somewhat of an increase in afternoon showers and thunderstorms, and caused the number of wildfires each day to diminish. On the 17th, the Arkansas Forestry Commission dropped the wildfire danger from Extreme to High. For the month of July, the Forestry Commission fought 430 fires, which consumed 8126 acres. A second heat wave occurred from the 17th through the 20th. During this time, numerous reporting stations rose above 100 degrees. A number of daily high temperatures records were broken, and additional daily records were broken for warmest low temperatures. The highest temperature recorded was 110 at Arkadelphia. Readings of 109 were observed at Russellville, Hot Springs airport, Cabot, and Morrilton. Stations reaching 108 included Little Rock, Mena, Dardanelle, Nimrod Dam, and Oden. Thereafter, temperatures moderated somewhat but still remained above normal. Another heat wave occurred from the 28th through the end of the month. During this period, the highest temperatures recorded were 112 at the Searcy airport, 111 at Little Rock, 110 at Cabot and Silver Hill, and 109 at Little Rock Air Force Base, the Batesville airport, Des Arc, and Evening Shade (forestry station).

Twenty stations recorded levels that were in their top ten lowest water levels since the last big agricultural drought in 2000. The U.S. Secretary of Agriculture designated almost all of Arkansas as being in a drought disaster. Tropical moisture began returning to Arkansas about the 6th of July. This brought higher humidities, somewhat of an increase in afternoon showers and thunderstorms, and caused the number of wildfires each day to diminish. On the 17th, the Arkansas Forestry Commission dropped the wildfire danger from Extreme to High. For the month of July, the Forestry Commission fought 430 fires, which consumed 8126 acres. A second heat wave occurred from the 17th through the 20th. During this time, numerous reporting stations rose above 100 degrees. A number of daily high temperatures records were broken, and additional daily records were broken for warmest low temperatures. The highest temperature recorded was 110 at Arkadelphia. Readings of 109 were observed at Russellville, Hot Springs airport, Cabot, and Morrilton. Stations reaching 108 included Little Rock, Mena, Dardanelle, Nimrod Dam, and Oden. Thereafter, temperatures moderated somewhat but still remained above normal. Another heat wave occurred from the 28th through the end of the month. During this period, the highest temperatures recorded were 112 at the Searcy airport, 111 at Little Rock, 110 at Cabot and Silver Hill, and 109 at Little Rock Air Force Base, the Batesville airport, Des Arc, and Evening Shade (forestry station).

Unusually hot weather that began in late June continued into the first part of July, thanks to a large area of high pressure aloft. Many high temperatures of 100 degrees or higher were recorded, and a number of daily high temperature records were broken. Although enough moisture returned such that very isolated showers and thunderstorms occurred each day, the rain did little to help the drought. Early in the month, about a third of Arkansas was affected by extreme drought, with severe drought existing in almost all other parts of the state. Pastures dried up, causing many cattle growers to send their livestock to market. Some stock ponds dried up, as well. Some water utilities instituted mandatory water restrictions. The hot, dry weather contributed to the spread of wildfires. At the beginning of the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission rated fire danger statewide as Extreme, a category which is used very rarely. High pressure, which had been nearly stationary over Arkansas, shifted to the west, allowing areas of low pressure aloft to move southeastward into Arkansas periodically. Scattered thunderstorms developed with each of the low pressure areas and isolated storms became severe on the 1st and 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning is believed to have started a house fire northeast of Albion. The house burned nearly to the ground. The sole resident, an 85 year-old woman, was killed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into Arkansas on the 5th, sparking a number of severe thunderstorms. On the 6th and 7th, the front had weakened but remained in the state, setting off a few more severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst struck downtown Hot Springs, doing considerable damage. A hotel had a large hole, approximately 30 feet by 20 feet, punched in its facade. A bank had windows blown out. A skywalk between two buildings was badly damaged with a lot of material hanging out of the bottom (over the street) and windows blown out. A motorcyclist suffered minor injuries after he took shelter under the skywalk and was hit by flying debris. A used car dealership in a 65 year-old building had one wall caved in and numerous windows blown out. Most of the cars that were for sale were damaged. A nearby tire shop had all of its front windows blown out. A theater had damage to its marquee and a large ceramic planter was blown through a wall. The cornice of one building was blown off, scattering bricks across an adjacent parking lot. A radio tower was bent over the roof of the central fire station. Another radio tower, belonging to an ambulance service, was also bent over. At least two dozen businesses and residences suffered roof damage. A number of very large trees were blown down. One of these fell against a house and was so large that a mobile crane was brought in to remove it. Numerous power lines were blown down, leaving thousands of people without power.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell in the downtown area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down onto U.S. 70.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought as August began. Unusual heat also continued into the beginning of the month. From the 1st through the 5th, high temperatures were 100 degrees or higher at many reporting stations. The highest temperatures during this time frame were 108 at Cabot and Silver Hill; 107 at Russellville, Conway, Booneville forestry station, and Oden; 106 at Little Rock, DeGray Lake State Park, Dardanelle, Subiaco, Morrilton, Leola, Malvern, Nimrod Dam, and Jessieville. After the first few days of the month, the high pressure aloft that had caused hot weather throughout July shifted to the west. This allowed at least some scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time, and heat indices gradually dropped below 105 in most areas. On the 3rd, a 16 month-old girl died in a hot car in Newport (Jackson Co.). According to police reports, the father was supposed to drop the baby off at daycare but forgot to do so. Thus, the baby remained in the car all day and was not discovered in the car until late afternoon. The high temperature at Newport that day reached 96 degrees and the heat index reached 106. The father was arrested at the scene and charged with negligent homicide.

Although Isaac was a hurricane in the Gulf of Mexico, it had weakened to a tropical depression by the time it reached Arkansas. Isaac passed through western Arkansas on the evening of the 30th and during the day on the 31st. Positions from the National Hurricane Center and the Hydrometeorological Prediction Center:  August 30th at 10 PM (CDT) 95 miles south-southwest of Little Rock, August 31st at 4 AM 95 miles west of Little Rock, August 31st at 10 AM 25 miles northeast of Fort Smith, and August 31st at 4 PM 40 miles east of Joplin, Missouri. Although Isaac had exited Arkansas by the end of the month, some effects, including bands of thunderstorms spiraling in toward its center, continued through September 1st into the wee hours of the 2nd. Isaac brought sustained winds of 23 to 33 mph and wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph in many parts of the state. The highest sustained wind in the Little Rock County Warning Area was 33 mph at Little Rock Air Force Base, and the strongest gust was 46 mph at the Pine Bluff airport. The strong winds over a prolonged period caused large numbers of trees to fall. Some of these trees had been weakened by the ongoing drought. Falling limbs and trees took down power lines, leaving more than 20,000 electric customers in the dark. Isaac also brought widespread rain. Three injuries were reported. Two people were injured in Little Rock when a tree fell on a car parked in a driveway; one person was injured in White Hall (Jefferson Co.) when a tree fell on a car. Where bands of showers and thunderstorms lingered over a particular area or where rain moved over the same area repeatedly, flash flooding occurred. Some of the larger storm totals included 11.29 inches at White Hall (Jefferson Co.), 10.70 inches at Griffithville (White Co.), 10.65 inches at the Pine Bluff Water Plant (Jefferson Co.), 8.90 inches at the Pine Bluff airport, 8.88 inches at Judsonia (White Co.), 8.40 inches at Steprock (White Co.), and 8.11 inches at Fordyce (Dallas Co.). The lowest barometric pressure reported in the Little Rock County Warning area was 29.51 inches at Camden. The combination of wind and rain caused damage in the rice crop, due to lodging of the rice. Cotton, corn, and soybeans appeared to have only minor damage. The rain improved prospects for the hay crop, and pastures providing forage for livestock improved.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall measured 5.09 inches in 24 hours.

A heat wave started on the 1st of the month and lasted through the 7th. The main effects were felt in central and southern Arkansas. Most high temperatures reached the upper 90s to above 100. The highest temperatures recorded included 105 at Booneville; 104 at Mena and Morrilton; 103 at Russellville, Searcy, Waldron, and Nimrod Dam; and 102 at Arkadelphia, Cabot, Clarksville, Dardanelle, Oden, and Silver Hill. Heat indices were frequently in the range of 105 to 110, with a few readings slightly above 110.

At the beginning of August, most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought. After a brief heat wave at the beginning of the month, the weather returned closer to a normal weather pattern, with weather systems bringing at least scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time. By the end of the month, there had been slight improvement in the drought in parts of western and central Arkansas. During the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought 425 fires, which burned 7721 acres. There were hundreds of additional, smaller fires fought by city fire departments and rural volunteer departments. The passage of Tropical Depression Isaac at the end of the month brought more substantial rainfall and consequent improvement in the drought. The drought improvement was reflected in the first Drought Monitor issued in September and will thus appear in September's Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Throughout the month, exceptional drought covered most of Woodruff County, except for the southeast where extreme drought existed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually hot weather that began in late June continued into the first part of July, thanks to a large area of high pressure aloft. Many high temperatures of 100 degrees or higher were recorded, and a number of daily high temperature records were broken. Although enough moisture returned such that very isolated showers and thunderstorms occurred each day, the rain did little to help the drought. Early in the month, about a third of Arkansas was affected by extreme drought, with severe drought existing in almost all other parts of the state. Pastures dried up, causing many cattle growers to send their livestock to market. Some stock ponds dried up, as well. Some water utilities instituted mandatory water restrictions. The hot, dry weather contributed to the spread of wildfires. At the beginning of the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission rated fire danger statewide as Extreme, a category which is used very rarely. County judges instituted burn bans for virtually every county in Arkansas. Sales of fireworks for the 4th of July were greatly affected, with some fireworks stands closing before the holiday. Some municipal fireworks shows were cancelled as a result of the dry weather. Dozens of fires broke out each day, but only a relatively small number of these exceeded 100 acres. On the 3rd, Arkansas Governor Mike Beebe authorized the National Guard to use Black Hawk helicopters to assist firefighters by dropping water on the larger fires using 660 gallon Bambi Buckets. On the 4th alone, the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought 43 wildfires which burned 1803 acres. During the first half of the month, many rivers in the state fell to unusually low levels. Twenty stations recorded levels that were in their top ten lowest water levels since the last big agricultural drought in 2000. The U.S. Secretary of Agriculture designated almost all of Arkansas as being in a drought disaster. Tropical moisture began returning to Arkansas about the 6th of July. This brought higher humidities, somewhat of an increase in afternoon showers and thunderstorms, and caused the number of wildfires each day to diminish. On the 17th, the Arkansas Forestry Commission dropped the wildfire danger from Extreme to High. For the month of July, the Forestry Commission fought 430 fires, which consumed 8126 acres. A second heat wave occurred from the 17th through the 20th. During this time, numerous reporting stations rose above 100 degrees. A number of daily high temperatures records were broken, and additional daily records were broken for warmest low temperatures. The highest temperature recorded was 110 at Arkadelphia. Readings of 109 were observed at Russellville, Hot Springs airport, Cabot, and Morrilton. Stations reaching 108 included Little Rock, Mena, Dardanelle, Nimrod Dam, and Oden. Thereafter, temperatures moderated somewhat but still remained above normal. Another heat wave occurred from the 28th through the end of the month. During this period, the highest temperatures recorded were 112 at the Searcy airport, 111 at Little Rock, 110 at Cabot and Silver Hill, and 109 at Little Rock Air Force Base, the Batesville airport, Des Arc, and Evening Shade (forestry station).

The Casa Fire was started by lightning 2 miles northwest of Casa in Perry County on the 13th and continued until the 15th. Altogether, 820 acres burned. A house, some outbuildings, a boat, and several vehicles were lost in the fire. A portion of Arkansas 155 was closed during the fire. At the height of the fire, homes in the town of Casa were evacuated, as well as homes in the communities of Homewood and Rose Creek. Yell and Conway counties assisted Perry County with the evacuations.

Schools in Pine Bluff dismissed early. The University of Arkansas at Pine Bluff cancelled afternoon classes. The county judge declared a disaster due to the flooding.

Isaac passed through western Arkansas on the evening of the 30th and during the day on the 31st. Positions from the National Hurricane Center and the Hydrometeorological Prediction Center:  August 30th at 10 PM (CDT) 95 miles south-southwest of Little Rock, August 31st at 4 AM 95 miles west of Little Rock, August 31st at 10 AM 25 miles northeast of Fort Smith, and August 31st at 4 PM 40 miles east of Joplin, Missouri. Although Isaac had exited Arkansas by the end of the month, some effects, including bands of thunderstorms spiraling in toward its center, continued through September 1st into the wee hours of the 2nd. Isaac brought sustained winds of 23 to 33 mph and wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph in many parts of the state. The highest sustained wind in the Little Rock County Warning Area was 33 mph at Little Rock Air Force Base, and the strongest gust was 46 mph at the Pine Bluff airport. The strong winds over a prolonged period caused large numbers of trees to fall. Some of these trees had been weakened by the ongoing drought. Falling limbs and trees took down power lines, leaving more than 20,000 electric customers in the dark. Isaac also brought widespread rain. Three injuries were reported. Two people were injured in Little Rock when a tree fell on a car parked in a driveway; one person was injured in White Hall (Jefferson Co.) when a tree fell on a car. Where bands of showers and thunderstorms lingered over a particular area or where rain moved over the same area repeatedly, flash flooding occurred. Some of the larger storm totals included 11.29 inches at White Hall (Jefferson Co.), 10.70 inches at Griffithville (White Co.), 10.65 inches at the Pine Bluff Water Plant (Jefferson Co.), 8.90 inches at the Pine Bluff airport, 8.88 inches at Judsonia (White Co.), 8.40 inches at Steprock (White Co.), and 8.11 inches at Fordyce (Dallas Co.). The lowest barometric pressure reported in the Little Rock County Warning area was 29.51 inches at Camden. The combination of wind and rain caused damage in the rice crop, due to lodging of the rice. Cotton, corn, and soybeans appeared to have only minor damage. The rain improved prospects for the hay crop, and pastures providing forage for livestock improved.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas around Tucker flooded, and flood waters approached Tucker Maximum Security Prison. Water seeped into the newly renovated chapel at the prison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although the remnants of Isaac were centered over Missouri on the 1st, showers and thunderstorms spiraling in toward Isaac's center affected most of Arkansas on the 1st and into the wee hours of the 2nd.  Isaac had been a hurricane in the Gulf of Mexico, but had weakened to a tropical depression by the time it reached Arkansas.

A heat wave started on the 1st of the month and lasted through the 7th. The main effects were felt in central and southern Arkansas. Most high temperatures reached the upper 90s to above 100. The highest temperatures recorded included 105 at Booneville; 104 at Mena and Morrilton; 103 at Russellville, Searcy, Waldron, and Nimrod Dam; and 102 at Arkadelphia, Cabot, Clarksville, Dardanelle, Oden, and Silver Hill. Heat indices were frequently in the range of 105 to 110, with a few readings slightly above 110.

At the beginning of August, most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought. After a brief heat wave at the beginning of the month, the weather returned closer to a normal weather pattern, with weather systems bringing at least scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time. By the end of the month, there had been slight improvement in the drought in parts of western and central Arkansas. During the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought 425 fires, which burned 7721 acres. There were hundreds of additional, smaller fires fought by city fire departments and rural volunteer departments. The passage of Tropical Depression Isaac at the end of the month brought more substantial rainfall and consequent improvement in the drought. The drought improvement was reflected in the first Drought Monitor issued in September and will thus appear in September's Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month, exceptional drought covered Hot Spring County, except for the southern part of the county where extreme drought existed. By the end of the month, conditions have improved slightly with extreme drought covering the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought as August began. Unusual heat also continued into the beginning of the month. From the 1st through the 5th, high temperatures were 100 degrees or higher at many reporting stations. The highest temperatures during this time frame were 108 at Cabot and Silver Hill; 107 at Russellville, Conway, Booneville forestry station, and Oden; 106 at Little Rock, DeGray Lake State Park, Dardanelle, Subiaco, Morrilton, Leola, Malvern, Nimrod Dam, and Jessieville. After the first few days of the month, the high pressure aloft that had caused hot weather throughout July shifted to the west. This allowed at least some scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time, and heat indices gradually dropped below 105 in most areas. On the 3rd, a 16 month-old girl died in a hot car in Newport (Jackson Co.). According to police reports, the father was supposed to drop the baby off at daycare but forgot to do so. Thus, the baby remained in the car all day and was not discovered in the car until late afternoon. The high temperature at Newport that day reached 96 degrees and the heat index reached 106. The father was arrested at the scene and charged with negligent homicide.

A cold front moved into Arkansas on the 5th, sparking a number of severe thunderstorms. On the 6th and 7th, the front had weakened but remained in the state, setting off a few more severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

Isaac brought sustained winds of 23 to 33 mph and wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph in many parts of the state. The highest sustained wind in the Little Rock County Warning Area was 33 mph at Little Rock Air Force Base, and the strongest gust was 46 mph at the Pine Bluff airport. The strong winds over a prolonged period caused large numbers of trees to fall. Some of these trees had been weakened by the ongoing drought. Falling limbs and trees took down power lines, leaving more than 20,000 electric customers in the dark. Isaac also brought widespread rain. Three injuries were reported. Two people were injured in Little Rock when a tree fell on a car parked in a driveway; one person was injured in White Hall (Jefferson Co.) when a tree fell on a car. Where bands of showers and thunderstorms lingered over a particular area or where rain moved over the same area repeatedly, flash flooding occurred. Some of the larger storm totals included 11.29 inches at White Hall (Jefferson Co.), 10.70 inches at Griffithville (White Co.), 10.65 inches at the Pine Bluff Water Plant (Jefferson Co.), 8.90 inches at the Pine Bluff airport, 8.88 inches at Judsonia (White Co.), 8.40 inches at Steprock (White Co.), and 8.11 inches at Fordyce (Dallas Co.). The lowest barometric pressure reported in the Little Rock County Warning area was 29.51 inches at Camden. The combination of wind and rain caused damage in the rice crop, due to lodging of the rice. Cotton, corn, and soybeans appeared to have only minor damage. The rain improved prospects for the hay crop, and pastures providing forage for livestock improved.

Most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought as August began. Unusual heat also continued into the beginning of the month. From the 1st through the 5th, high temperatures were 100 degrees or higher at many reporting stations. The highest temperatures during this time frame were 108 at Cabot and Silver Hill; 107 at Russellville, Conway, Booneville forestry station, and Oden; 106 at Little Rock, DeGray Lake State Park, Dardanelle, Subiaco, Morrilton, Leola, Malvern, Nimrod Dam, and Jessieville. After the first few days of the month, the high pressure aloft that had caused hot weather throughout July shifted to the west. This allowed at least some scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time, and heat indices gradually dropped below 105 in most areas. On the 3rd, a 16 month-old girl died in a hot car in Newport (Jackson Co.). According to police reports, the father was supposed to drop the baby off at daycare but forgot to do so. Thus, the baby remained in the car all day and was not discovered in the car until late afternoon. The high temperature at Newport that day reached 96 degrees and the heat index reached 106. The father was arrested at the scene and charged with negligent homicide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave started on the 1st of the month and lasted through the 7th. The main effects were felt in central and southern Arkansas. Most high temperatures reached the upper 90s to above 100. The highest temperatures recorded included 105 at Booneville; 104 at Mena and Morrilton; 103 at Russellville, Searcy, Waldron, and Nimrod Dam; and 102 at Arkadelphia, Cabot, Clarksville, Dardanelle, Oden, and Silver Hill. Heat indices were frequently in the range of 105 to 110, with a few readings slightly above 110.

At the beginning of August, most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought. After a brief heat wave at the beginning of the month, the weather returned closer to a normal weather pattern, with weather systems bringing at least scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time. By the end of the month, there had been slight improvement in the drought in parts of western and central Arkansas. During the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought 425 fires, which burned 7721 acres. There were hundreds of additional, smaller fires fought by city fire departments and rural volunteer departments. The passage of Tropical Depression Isaac at the end of the month brought more substantial rainfall and consequent improvement in the drought. The drought improvement was reflected in the first Drought Monitor issued in September and will thus appear in September's Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month, extreme drought covered all of Jefferson County, except for the southeastern tip where severe drought existed. By the end of the month, conditions had improved somewhat with extreme drought over the northern part of the county and severe drought elsewhere.

Although Isaac was a hurricane in the Gulf of Mexico, it had weakened to a tropical depression by the time it reached Arkansas. Isaac passed through western Arkansas on the evening of the 30th and during the day on the 31st. Positions from the National Hurricane Center and the Hydrometeorological Prediction Center:  August 30th at 10 PM (CDT) 95 miles south-southwest of Little Rock, August 31st at 4 AM 95 miles west of Little Rock, August 31st at 10 AM 25 miles northeast of Fort Smith, and August 31st at 4 PM 40 miles east of Joplin, Missouri. Although Isaac had exited Arkansas by the end of the month, some effects, including bands of thunderstorms spiraling in toward its center, continued through September 1st into the wee hours of the 2nd. Isaac brought sustained winds of 23 to 33 mph and wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph in many parts of the state. The highest sustained wind in the Little Rock County Warning Area was 33 mph at Little Rock Air Force Base, and the strongest gust was 46 mph at the Pine Bluff airport. The strong winds over a prolonged period caused large numbers of trees to fall. Some of these trees had been weakened by the ongoing drought. Falling limbs and trees took down power lines, leaving more than 20,000 electric customers in the dark. Isaac also brought widespread rain. Three injuries were reported. Two people were injured in Little Rock when a tree fell on a car parked in a driveway; one person was injured in White Hall (Jefferson Co.) when a tree fell on a car. Where bands of showers and thunderstorms lingered over a particular area or where rain moved over the same area repeatedly, flash flooding occurred. Some of the larger storm totals included 11.29 inches at White Hall (Jefferson Co.), 10.70 inches at Griffithville (White Co.), 10.65 inches at the Pine Bluff Water Plant (Jefferson Co.), 8.90 inches at the Pine Bluff airport, 8.88 inches at Judsonia (White Co.), 8.40 inches at Steprock (White Co.), and 8.11 inches at Fordyce (Dallas Co.). The lowest barometric pressure reported in the Little Rock County Warning area was 29.51 inches at Camden. The combination of wind and rain caused damage in the rice crop, due to lodging of the rice. Cotton, corn, and soybeans appeared to have only minor damage. The rain improved prospects for the hay crop, and pastures providing forage for livestock improved.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave started on the 1st of the month and lasted through the 7th. The main effects were felt in central and southern Arkansas. Most high temperatures reached the upper 90s to above 100. The highest temperatures recorded included 105 at Booneville; 104 at Mena and Morrilton; 103 at Russellville, Searcy, Waldron, and Nimrod Dam; and 102 at Arkadelphia, Cabot, Clarksville, Dardanelle, Oden, and Silver Hill. Heat indices were frequently in the range of 105 to 110, with a few readings slightly above 110.

At the beginning of August, most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought. After a brief heat wave at the beginning of the month, the weather returned closer to a normal weather pattern, with weather systems bringing at least scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time. By the end of the month, there had been slight improvement in the drought in parts of western and central Arkansas. During the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought 425 fires, which burned 7721 acres. There were hundreds of additional, smaller fires fought by city fire departments and rural volunteer departments. The passage of Tropical Depression Isaac at the end of the month brought more substantial rainfall and consequent improvement in the drought. The drought improvement was reflected in the first Drought Monitor issued in September and will thus appear in September's Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month, extreme drought covered the northern part of Cleveland County with severe drought elsewhere. By the end of the month, conditions had improved somewhat with severe drought covering the entire county.

A heat wave started on the 1st of the month and lasted through the 7th. The main effects were felt in central and southern Arkansas. Most high temperatures reached the upper 90s to above 100. The highest temperatures recorded included 105 at Booneville; 104 at Mena and Morrilton; 103 at Russellville, Searcy, Waldron, and Nimrod Dam; and 102 at Arkadelphia, Cabot, Clarksville, Dardanelle, Oden, and Silver Hill. Heat indices were frequently in the range of 105 to 110, with a few readings slightly above 110.

Most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought as August began. Unusual heat also continued into the beginning of the month. From the 1st through the 5th, high temperatures were 100 degrees or higher at many reporting stations. The highest temperatures during this time frame were 108 at Cabot and Silver Hill; 107 at Russellville, Conway, Booneville forestry station, and Oden; 106 at Little Rock, DeGray Lake State Park, Dardanelle, Subiaco, Morrilton, Leola, Malvern, Nimrod Dam, and Jessieville. After the first few days of the month, the high pressure aloft that had caused hot weather throughout July shifted to the west. This allowed at least some scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time, and heat indices gradually dropped below 105 in most areas. On the 3rd, a 16 month-old girl died in a hot car in Newport (Jackson Co.). According to police reports, the father was supposed to drop the baby off at daycare but forgot to do so. Thus, the baby remained in the car all day and was not discovered in the car until late afternoon. The high temperature at Newport that day reached 96 degrees and the heat index reached 106. The father was arrested at the scene and charged with negligent homicide.

A large cluster of thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front during the wee hours of the 13th. The storms had their biggest effect down the Arkansas River Valley. Impressive rainfall amounts occurred in parts of central Arkansas, especially considering the area was in an exceptional drought. Additional storms developed during the late afternoon and evening along the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall measured 2.67 inches in 4 hours.

Positions from the National Hurricane Center and the Hydrometeorological Prediction Center:  August 30th at 10 PM (CDT) 95 miles south-southwest of Little Rock, August 31st at 4 AM 95 miles west of Little Rock, August 31st at 10 AM 25 miles northeast of Fort Smith, and August 31st at 4 PM 40 miles east of Joplin, Missouri. Although Isaac had exited Arkansas by the end of the month, some effects, including bands of thunderstorms spiraling in toward its center, continued through September 1st into the wee hours of the 2nd. Isaac brought sustained winds of 23 to 33 mph and wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph in many parts of the state. The highest sustained wind in the Little Rock County Warning Area was 33 mph at Little Rock Air Force Base, and the strongest gust was 46 mph at the Pine Bluff airport. The strong winds over a prolonged period caused large numbers of trees to fall. Some of these trees had been weakened by the ongoing drought. Falling limbs and trees took down power lines, leaving more than 20,000 electric customers in the dark. Isaac also brought widespread rain. Three injuries were reported. Two people were injured in Little Rock when a tree fell on a car parked in a driveway; one person was injured in White Hall (Jefferson Co.) when a tree fell on a car. Where bands of showers and thunderstorms lingered over a particular area or where rain moved over the same area repeatedly, flash flooding occurred. Some of the larger storm totals included 11.29 inches at White Hall (Jefferson Co.), 10.70 inches at Griffithville (White Co.), 10.65 inches at the Pine Bluff Water Plant (Jefferson Co.), 8.90 inches at the Pine Bluff airport, 8.88 inches at Judsonia (White Co.), 8.40 inches at Steprock (White Co.), and 8.11 inches at Fordyce (Dallas Co.). The lowest barometric pressure reported in the Little Rock County Warning area was 29.51 inches at Camden. The combination of wind and rain caused damage in the rice crop, due to lodging of the rice. Cotton, corn, and soybeans appeared to have only minor damage. The rain improved prospects for the hay crop, and pastures providing forage for livestock improved.

Although the remnants of Isaac were centered over Missouri on the 1st, showers and thunderstorms spiraling in toward Isaac's center affected most of Arkansas on the 1st and into the wee hours of the 2nd.  Isaac had been a hurricane in the Gulf of Mexico, but had weakened to a tropical depression by the time it reached Arkansas.

At the beginning of August, most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought. After a brief heat wave at the beginning of the month, the weather returned closer to a normal weather pattern, with weather systems bringing at least scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time. By the end of the month, there had been slight improvement in the drought in parts of western and central Arkansas. During the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought 425 fires, which burned 7721 acres. There were hundreds of additional, smaller fires fought by city fire departments and rural volunteer departments. The passage of Tropical Depression Isaac at the end of the month brought more substantial rainfall and consequent improvement in the drought. The drought improvement was reflected in the first Drought Monitor issued in September and will thus appear in September's Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Throughout the month, parts of western Bradley County were in extreme drought, with severe drought elsewhere.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave started on the 1st of the month and lasted through the 7th. The main effects were felt in central and southern Arkansas. Most high temperatures reached the upper 90s to above 100. The highest temperatures recorded included 105 at Booneville; 104 at Mena and Morrilton; 103 at Russellville, Searcy, Waldron, and Nimrod Dam; and 102 at Arkadelphia, Cabot, Clarksville, Dardanelle, Oden, and Silver Hill. Heat indices were frequently in the range of 105 to 110, with a few readings slightly above 110.

The highest sustained wind in the Little Rock County Warning Area was 33 mph at Little Rock Air Force Base, and the strongest gust was 46 mph at the Pine Bluff airport. The strong winds over a prolonged period caused large numbers of trees to fall. Some of these trees had been weakened by the ongoing drought. Falling limbs and trees took down power lines, leaving more than 20,000 electric customers in the dark. Isaac also brought widespread rain. Three injuries were reported. Two people were injured in Little Rock when a tree fell on a car parked in a driveway; one person was injured in White Hall (Jefferson Co.) when a tree fell on a car. Where bands of showers and thunderstorms lingered over a particular area or where rain moved over the same area repeatedly, flash flooding occurred. Some of the larger storm totals included 11.29 inches at White Hall (Jefferson Co.), 10.70 inches at Griffithville (White Co.), 10.65 inches at the Pine Bluff Water Plant (Jefferson Co.), 8.90 inches at the Pine Bluff airport, 8.88 inches at Judsonia (White Co.), 8.40 inches at Steprock (White Co.), and 8.11 inches at Fordyce (Dallas Co.). The lowest barometric pressure reported in the Little Rock County Warning area was 29.51 inches at Camden. The combination of wind and rain caused damage in the rice crop, due to lodging of the rice. Cotton, corn, and soybeans appeared to have only minor damage. The rain improved prospects for the hay crop, and pastures providing forage for livestock improved.

Most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought as August began. Unusual heat also continued into the beginning of the month. From the 1st through the 5th, high temperatures were 100 degrees or higher at many reporting stations. The highest temperatures during this time frame were 108 at Cabot and Silver Hill; 107 at Russellville, Conway, Booneville forestry station, and Oden; 106 at Little Rock, DeGray Lake State Park, Dardanelle, Subiaco, Morrilton, Leola, Malvern, Nimrod Dam, and Jessieville. After the first few days of the month, the high pressure aloft that had caused hot weather throughout July shifted to the west. This allowed at least some scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time, and heat indices gradually dropped below 105 in most areas. On the 3rd, a 16 month-old girl died in a hot car in Newport (Jackson Co.). According to police reports, the father was supposed to drop the baby off at daycare but forgot to do so. Thus, the baby remained in the car all day and was not discovered in the car until late afternoon. The high temperature at Newport that day reached 96 degrees and the heat index reached 106. The father was arrested at the scene and charged with negligent homicide.

At the beginning of August, most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought. After a brief heat wave at the beginning of the month, the weather returned closer to a normal weather pattern, with weather systems bringing at least scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time. By the end of the month, there had been slight improvement in the drought in parts of western and central Arkansas. During the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought 425 fires, which burned 7721 acres. There were hundreds of additional, smaller fires fought by city fire departments and rural volunteer departments. The passage of Tropical Depression Isaac at the end of the month brought more substantial rainfall and consequent improvement in the drought. The drought improvement was reflected in the first Drought Monitor issued in September and will thus appear in September's Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional drought covered Jackson County throughout the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually hot weather that began in late June continued into the first part of July, thanks to a large area of high pressure aloft. Many high temperatures of 100 degrees or higher were recorded, and a number of daily high temperature records were broken. Although enough moisture returned such that very isolated showers and thunderstorms occurred each day, the rain did little to help the drought. Early in the month, about a third of Arkansas was affected by extreme drought, with severe drought existing in almost all other parts of the state. Pastures dried up, causing many cattle growers to send their livestock to market. Some stock ponds dried up, as well. Some water utilities instituted mandatory water restrictions. The hot, dry weather contributed to the spread of wildfires. At the beginning of the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission rated fire danger statewide as Extreme, a category which is used very rarely. County judges instituted burn bans for virtually every county in Arkansas. Sales of fireworks for the 4th of July were greatly affected, with some fireworks stands closing before the holiday. Some municipal fireworks shows were cancelled as a result of the dry weather. Dozens of fires broke out each day, but only a relatively small number of these exceeded 100 acres. On the 3rd, Arkansas Governor Mike Beebe authorized the National Guard to use Black Hawk helicopters to assist firefighters by dropping water on the larger fires using 660 gallon Bambi Buckets. On the 4th alone, the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought 43 wildfires which burned 1803 acres. During the first half of the month, many rivers in the state fell to unusually low levels. Twenty stations recorded levels that were in their top ten lowest water levels since the last big agricultural drought in 2000. The U.S. Secretary of Agriculture designated almost all of Arkansas as being in a drought disaster. Tropical moisture began returning to Arkansas about the 6th of July. This brought higher humidities, somewhat of an increase in afternoon showers and thunderstorms, and caused the number of wildfires each day to diminish. On the 17th, the Arkansas Forestry Commission dropped the wildfire danger from Extreme to High. For the month of July, the Forestry Commission fought 430 fires, which consumed 8126 acres. A second heat wave occurred from the 17th through the 20th. During this time, numerous reporting stations rose above 100 degrees. A number of daily high temperatures records were broken, and additional daily records were broken for warmest low temperatures. The highest temperature recorded was 110 at Arkadelphia. Readings of 109 were observed at Russellville, Hot Springs airport, Cabot, and Morrilton. Stations reaching 108 included Little Rock, Mena, Dardanelle, Nimrod Dam, and Oden. Thereafter, temperatures moderated somewhat but still remained above normal. Another heat wave occurred from the 28th through the end of the month. During this period, the highest temperatures recorded were 112 at the Searcy airport, 111 at Little Rock, 110 at Cabot and Silver Hill, and 109 at Little Rock Air Force Base, the Batesville airport, Des Arc, and Evening Shade (forestry station).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of July, severe drought covered Saline County. By the end of the month, conditions had worsened, with exceptional drought covering the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave started on the 1st of the month and lasted through the 7th. The main effects were felt in central and southern Arkansas. Most high temperatures reached the upper 90s to above 100. The highest temperatures recorded included 105 at Booneville; 104 at Mena and Morrilton; 103 at Russellville, Searcy, Waldron, and Nimrod Dam; and 102 at Arkadelphia, Cabot, Clarksville, Dardanelle, Oden, and Silver Hill. Heat indices were frequently in the range of 105 to 110, with a few readings slightly above 110.

The U.S. Secretary of Agriculture designated almost all of Arkansas as being in a drought disaster. Tropical moisture began returning to Arkansas about the 6th of July. This brought higher humidities, somewhat of an increase in afternoon showers and thunderstorms, and caused the number of wildfires each day to diminish. On the 17th, the Arkansas Forestry Commission dropped the wildfire danger from Extreme to High. For the month of July, the Forestry Commission fought 430 fires, which consumed 8126 acres. A second heat wave occurred from the 17th through the 20th. During this time, numerous reporting stations rose above 100 degrees. A number of daily high temperatures records were broken, and additional daily records were broken for warmest low temperatures. The highest temperature recorded was 110 at Arkadelphia. Readings of 109 were observed at Russellville, Hot Springs airport, Cabot, and Morrilton. Stations reaching 108 included Little Rock, Mena, Dardanelle, Nimrod Dam, and Oden. Thereafter, temperatures moderated somewhat but still remained above normal. Another heat wave occurred from the 28th through the end of the month. During this period, the highest temperatures recorded were 112 at the Searcy airport, 111 at Little Rock, 110 at Cabot and Silver Hill, and 109 at Little Rock Air Force Base, the Batesville airport, Des Arc, and Evening Shade (forestry station).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very well-developed dust devil damaged four chicken houses near Delaware. Tin was torn off the roof and sides of the chicken houses. The debris was pulled so high into the air that it flew over trees and ridges. Cattle and horses were frightened and ran.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually hot weather that began in late June continued into the first part of July, thanks to a large area of high pressure aloft. Many high temperatures of 100 degrees or higher were recorded, and a number of daily high temperature records were broken. Although enough moisture returned such that very isolated showers and thunderstorms occurred each day, the rain did little to help the drought. Early in the month, about a third of Arkansas was affected by extreme drought, with severe drought existing in almost all other parts of the state. Pastures dried up, causing many cattle growers to send their livestock to market. Some stock ponds dried up, as well. Some water utilities instituted mandatory water restrictions. The hot, dry weather contributed to the spread of wildfires. At the beginning of the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission rated fire danger statewide as Extreme, a category which is used very rarely. County judges instituted burn bans for virtually every county in Arkansas. Sales of fireworks for the 4th of July were greatly affected, with some fireworks stands closing before the holiday. Some municipal fireworks shows were cancelled as a result of the dry weather. Dozens of fires broke out each day, but only a relatively small number of these exceeded 100 acres. On the 3rd, Arkansas Governor Mike Beebe authorized the National Guard to use Black Hawk helicopters to assist firefighters by dropping water on the larger fires using 660 gallon Bambi Buckets. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of August, most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought. After a brief heat wave at the beginning of the month, the weather returned closer to a normal weather pattern, with weather systems bringing at least scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time. By the end of the month, there had been slight improvement in the drought in parts of western and central Arkansas. During the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought 425 fires, which burned 7721 acres. There were hundreds of additional, smaller fires fought by city fire departments and rural volunteer departments. The passage of Tropical Depression Isaac at the end of the month brought more substantial rainfall and consequent improvement in the drought. The drought improvement was reflected in the first Drought Monitor issued in September and will thus appear in September's Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Throughout the month, severe drought covered the western half of Desha County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave started on the 1st of the month and lasted through the 7th. The main effects were felt in central and southern Arkansas. Most high temperatures reached the upper 90s to above 100. The highest temperatures recorded included 105 at Booneville; 104 at Mena and Morrilton; 103 at Russellville, Searcy, Waldron, and Nimrod Dam; and 102 at Arkadelphia, Cabot, Clarksville, Dardanelle, Oden, and Silver Hill. Heat indices were frequently in the range of 105 to 110, with a few readings slightly above 110.

A heat wave started on the 1st of the month and lasted through the 7th. The main effects were felt in central and southern Arkansas. Most high temperatures reached the upper 90s to above 100. The highest temperatures recorded included 105 at Booneville; 104 at Mena and Morrilton; 103 at Russellville, Searcy, Waldron, and Nimrod Dam; and 102 at Arkadelphia, Cabot, Clarksville, Dardanelle, Oden, and Silver Hill. Heat indices were frequently in the range of 105 to 110, with a few readings slightly above 110.

Most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought as August began. Unusual heat also continued into the beginning of the month. From the 1st through the 5th, high temperatures were 100 degrees or higher at many reporting stations. The highest temperatures during this time frame were 108 at Cabot and Silver Hill; 107 at Russellville, Conway, Booneville forestry station, and Oden; 106 at Little Rock, DeGray Lake State Park, Dardanelle, Subiaco, Morrilton, Leola, Malvern, Nimrod Dam, and Jessieville. After the first few days of the month, the high pressure aloft that had caused hot weather throughout July shifted to the west. This allowed at least some scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time, and heat indices gradually dropped below 105 in most areas. On the 3rd, a 16 month-old girl died in a hot car in Newport (Jackson Co.). According to police reports, the father was supposed to drop the baby off at daycare but forgot to do so. Thus, the baby remained in the car all day and was not discovered in the car until late afternoon. The high temperature at Newport that day reached 96 degrees and the heat index reached 106. The father was arrested at the scene and charged with negligent homicide.

Although Isaac was a hurricane in the Gulf of Mexico, it had weakened to a tropical depression by the time it reached Arkansas. Isaac passed through western Arkansas on the evening of the 30th and during the day on the 31st. Positions from the National Hurricane Center and the Hydrometeorological Prediction Center:  August 30th at 10 PM (CDT) 95 miles south-southwest of Little Rock, August 31st at 4 AM 95 miles west of Little Rock, August 31st at 10 AM 25 miles northeast of Fort Smith, and August 31st at 4 PM 40 miles east of Joplin, Missouri. Although Isaac had exited Arkansas by the end of the month, some effects, including bands of thunderstorms spiraling in toward its center, continued through September 1st into the wee hours of the 2nd. Isaac brought sustained winds of 23 to 33 mph and wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph in many parts of the state. The highest sustained wind in the Little Rock County Warning Area was 33 mph at Little Rock Air Force Base, and the strongest gust was 46 mph at the Pine Bluff airport. The strong winds over a prolonged period caused large numbers of trees to fall. Some of these trees had been weakened by the ongoing drought. Falling limbs and trees took down power lines, leaving more than 20,000 electric customers in the dark. Isaac also brought widespread rain. Three injuries were reported. Two people were injured in Little Rock when a tree fell on a car parked in a driveway; one person was injured in White Hall (Jefferson Co.) when a tree fell on a car. Where bands of showers and thunderstorms lingered over a particular area or where rain moved over the same area repeatedly, flash flooding occurred. Some of the larger storm totals included 11.29 inches at White Hall (Jefferson Co.), 10.70 inches at Griffithville (White Co.), 10.65 inches at the Pine Bluff Water Plant (Jefferson Co.), 8.90 inches at the Pine Bluff airport, 8.88 inches at Judsonia (White Co.), 8.40 inches at Steprock (White Co.), and 8.11 inches at Fordyce (Dallas Co.). The lowest barometric pressure reported in the Little Rock County Warning area was 29.51 inches at Camden. The combination of wind and rain caused damage in the rice crop, due to lodging of the rice. Cotton, corn, and soybeans appeared to have only minor damage. The rain improved prospects for the hay crop, and pastures providing forage for livestock improved.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of August, most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought. After a brief heat wave at the beginning of the month, the weather returned closer to a normal weather pattern, with weather systems bringing at least scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time. By the end of the month, there had been slight improvement in the drought in parts of western and central Arkansas. During the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought 425 fires, which burned 7721 acres. There were hundreds of additional, smaller fires fought by city fire departments and rural volunteer departments. The passage of Tropical Depression Isaac at the end of the month brought more substantial rainfall and consequent improvement in the drought. The drought improvement was reflected in the first Drought Monitor issued in September and will thus appear in September's Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional drought covered Boone County throughout the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought as August began. Unusual heat also continued into the beginning of the month. From the 1st through the 5th, high temperatures were 100 degrees or higher at many reporting stations. The highest temperatures during this time frame were 108 at Cabot and Silver Hill; 107 at Russellville, Conway, Booneville forestry station, and Oden; 106 at Little Rock, DeGray Lake State Park, Dardanelle, Subiaco, Morrilton, Leola, Malvern, Nimrod Dam, and Jessieville. After the first few days of the month, the high pressure aloft that had caused hot weather throughout July shifted to the west. This allowed at least some scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time, and heat indices gradually dropped below 105 in most areas. On the 3rd, a 16 month-old girl died in a hot car in Newport (Jackson Co.). According to police reports, the father was supposed to drop the baby off at daycare but forgot to do so. Thus, the baby remained in the car all day and was not discovered in the car until late afternoon. The high temperature at Newport that day reached 96 degrees and the heat index reached 106. The father was arrested at the scene and charged with negligent homicide.

Although Isaac was a hurricane in the Gulf of Mexico, it had weakened to a tropical depression by the time it reached Arkansas. Isaac passed through western Arkansas on the evening of the 30th and during the day on the 31st. Positions from the National Hurricane Center and the Hydrometeorological Prediction Center:  August 30th at 10 PM (CDT) 95 miles south-southwest of Little Rock, August 31st at 4 AM 95 miles west of Little Rock, August 31st at 10 AM 25 miles northeast of Fort Smith, and August 31st at 4 PM 40 miles east of Joplin, Missouri. Although Isaac had exited Arkansas by the end of the month, some effects, including bands of thunderstorms spiraling in toward its center, continued through September 1st into the wee hours of the 2nd. Isaac brought sustained winds of 23 to 33 mph and wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph in many parts of the state. The highest sustained wind in the Little Rock County Warning Area was 33 mph at Little Rock Air Force Base, and the strongest gust was 46 mph at the Pine Bluff airport. The strong winds over a prolonged period caused large numbers of trees to fall. Some of these trees had been weakened by the ongoing drought. Falling limbs and trees took down power lines, leaving more than 20,000 electric customers in the dark. Isaac also brought widespread rain. Three injuries were reported. Two people were injured in Little Rock when a tree fell on a car parked in a driveway; one person was injured in White Hall (Jefferson Co.) when a tree fell on a car. Where bands of showers and thunderstorms lingered over a particular area or where rain moved over the same area repeatedly, flash flooding occurred. Some of the larger storm totals included 11.29 inches at White Hall (Jefferson Co.), 10.70 inches at Griffithville (White Co.), 10.65 inches at the Pine Bluff Water Plant (Jefferson Co.), 8.90 inches at the Pine Bluff airport, 8.88 inches at Judsonia (White Co.), 8.40 inches at Steprock (White Co.), and 8.11 inches at Fordyce (Dallas Co.). The lowest barometric pressure reported in the Little Rock County Warning area was 29.51 inches at Camden. The combination of wind and rain caused damage in the rice crop, due to lodging of the rice. Cotton, corn, and soybeans appeared to have only minor damage. The rain improved prospects for the hay crop, and pastures providing forage for livestock improved.

Some of the larger storm totals included 11.29 inches at White Hall (Jefferson Co.), 10.70 inches at Griffithville (White Co.), 10.65 inches at the Pine Bluff Water Plant (Jefferson Co.), 8.90 inches at the Pine Bluff airport, 8.88 inches at Judsonia (White Co.), 8.40 inches at Steprock (White Co.), and 8.11 inches at Fordyce (Dallas Co.). The lowest barometric pressure reported in the Little Rock County Warning area was 29.51 inches at Camden. The combination of wind and rain caused damage in the rice crop, due to lodging of the rice. Cotton, corn, and soybeans appeared to have only minor damage. The rain improved prospects for the hay crop, and pastures providing forage for livestock improved.

Although Isaac was a hurricane in the Gulf of Mexico, it had weakened to a tropical depression by the time it reached Arkansas. Isaac passed through western Arkansas on the evening of the 30th and during the day on the 31st. Positions from the National Hurricane Center and the Hydrometeorological Prediction Center:  August 30th at 10 PM (CDT) 95 miles south-southwest of Little Rock, August 31st at 4 AM 95 miles west of Little Rock, August 31st at 10 AM 25 miles northeast of Fort Smith, and August 31st at 4 PM 40 miles east of Joplin, Missouri. Although Isaac had exited Arkansas by the end of the month, some effects, including bands of thunderstorms spiraling in toward its center, continued through September 1st into the wee hours of the 2nd. Isaac brought sustained winds of 23 to 33 mph and wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph in many parts of the state. The highest sustained wind in the Little Rock County Warning Area was 33 mph at Little Rock Air Force Base, and the strongest gust was 46 mph at the Pine Bluff airport. The strong winds over a prolonged period caused large numbers of trees to fall. Some of these trees had been weakened by the ongoing drought. Falling limbs and trees took down power lines, leaving more than 20,000 electric customers in the dark. Isaac also brought widespread rain. Three injuries were reported. Two people were injured in Little Rock when a tree fell on a car parked in a driveway; one person was injured in White Hall (Jefferson Co.) when a tree fell on a car. A cold front moved through Arkansas on the 7th, causing numerous severe thunderstorms to occur. Wind damage was widespread. Approximately 80,000 electric customers lost service; this was about four times the number of outages that Tropical Depression Isaac produced in Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down onto Arkansas 133.

At the beginning of August, most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought. After a brief heat wave at the beginning of the month, the weather returned closer to a normal weather pattern, with weather systems bringing at least scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time. By the end of the month, there had been slight improvement in the drought in parts of western and central Arkansas. During the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought 425 fires, which burned 7721 acres. There were hundreds of additional, smaller fires fought by city fire departments and rural volunteer departments. The passage of Tropical Depression Isaac at the end of the month brought more substantial rainfall and consequent improvement in the drought. The drought improvement was reflected in the first Drought Monitor issued in September and will thus appear in September's Storm Data.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of the month, extreme drought covered most of Polk County, except for exceptional drought in the southeastern part of the county. By the end of the month, conditions had improved slightly -- extreme drought covered most of the county, except for severe drought in the southwest corner.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually hot weather that began in late June continued into the first part of July, thanks to a large area of high pressure aloft. Many high temperatures of 100 degrees or higher were recorded, and a number of daily high temperature records were broken. Although enough moisture returned such that very isolated showers and thunderstorms occurred each day, the rain did little to help the drought. Early in the month, about a third of Arkansas was affected by extreme drought, with severe drought existing in almost all other parts of the state. Pastures dried up, causing many cattle growers to send their livestock to market. Some stock ponds dried up, as well. Some water utilities instituted mandatory water restrictions. The hot, dry weather contributed to the spread of wildfires. At the beginning of the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission rated fire danger statewide as Extreme, a category which is used very rarely. County judges instituted burn bans for virtually every county in Arkansas. Sales of fireworks for the 4th of July were greatly affected, with some fireworks stands closing before the holiday. Some municipal fireworks shows were cancelled as a result of the dry weather. Dozens of fires broke out each day, but only a relatively small number of these exceeded 100 acres. On the 3rd, Arkansas Governor Mike Beebe authorized the National Guard to use Black Hawk helicopters to assist firefighters by dropping water on the larger fires using 660 gallon Bambi Buckets. On the 4th alone, the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought 43 wildfires which burned 1803 acres. During the first half of the month, many rivers in the state fell to unusually low levels. Twenty stations recorded levels that were in their top ten lowest water levels since the last big agricultural drought in 2000. The U.S. Secretary of Agriculture designated almost all of Arkansas as being in a drought disaster. Tropical moisture began returning to Arkansas about the 6th of July. This brought higher humidities, somewhat of an increase in afternoon showers and thunderstorms, and caused the number of wildfires each day to diminish. On the 17th, the Arkansas Forestry Commission dropped the wildfire danger from Extreme to High. For the month of July, the Forestry Commission fought 430 fires, which consumed 8126 acres. A second heat wave occurred from the 17th through the 20th. During this time, numerous reporting stations rose above 100 degrees. A number of daily high temperatures records were broken, and additional daily records were broken for warmest low temperatures. The highest temperature recorded was 110 at Arkadelphia. Readings of 109 were observed at Russellville, Hot Springs airport, Cabot, and Morrilton. Stations reaching 108 included Little Rock, Mena, Dardanelle, Nimrod Dam, and Oden. Thereafter, temperatures moderated somewhat but still remained above normal. Another heat wave occurred from the 28th through the end of the month. During this period, the highest temperatures recorded were 112 at the Searcy airport, 111 at Little Rock, 110 at Cabot and Silver Hill, and 109 at Little Rock Air Force Base, the Batesville airport, Des Arc, and Evening Shade (forestry station).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of July, extreme drought covered much of Van Buren County. By the end of the month, conditions had worsened, with exceptional drought covering the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Most of Arkansas was under extreme or exceptional drought as August began. Unusual heat also continued into the beginning of the month. From the 1st through the 5th, high temperatures were 100 degrees or higher at many reporting stations. The highest temperatures during this time frame were 108 at Cabot and Silver Hill; 107 at Russellville, Conway, Booneville forestry station, and Oden; 106 at Little Rock, DeGray Lake State Park, Dardanelle, Subiaco, Morrilton, Leola, Malvern, Nimrod Dam, and Jessieville. After the first few days of the month, the high pressure aloft that had caused hot weather throughout July shifted to the west. This allowed at least some scattered showers and thunderstorms from time to time, and heat indices gradually dropped below 105 in most areas. On the 3rd, a 16 month-old girl died in a hot car in Newport (Jackson Co.). According to police reports, the father was supposed to drop the baby off at daycare but forgot to do so. Thus, the baby remained in the car all day and was not discovered in the car until late afternoon. The high temperature at Newport that day reached 96 degrees and the heat index reached 106. The father was arrested at the scene and charged with negligent homicide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually hot weather that began in late June continued into the first part of July, thanks to a large area of high pressure aloft. Many high temperatures of 100 degrees or higher were recorded, and a number of daily high temperature records were broken. Although enough moisture returned such that very isolated showers and thunderstorms occurred each day, the rain did little to help the drought. Early in the month, about a third of Arkansas was affected by extreme drought, with severe drought existing in almost all other parts of the state. Pastures dried up, causing many cattle growers to send their livestock to market. Some stock ponds dried up, as well. Some water utilities instituted mandatory water restrictions. The hot, dry weather contributed to the spread of wildfires. At the beginning of the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission rated fire danger statewide as Extreme, a category which is used very rarely. County judges instituted burn bans for virtually every county in Arkansas. Sales of fireworks for the 4th of July were greatly affected, with some fireworks stands closing before the holiday. Some municipal fireworks shows were cancelled as a result of the dry weather. Dozens of fires broke out each day, but only a relatively small number of these exceeded 100 acres. On the 3rd, Arkansas Governor Mike Beebe authorized the National Guard to use Black Hawk helicopters to assist firefighters by dropping water on the larger fires using 660 gallon Bambi Buckets. On the 4th alone, the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought 43 wildfires which burned 1803 acres. During the first half of the month, many rivers in the state fell to unusually low levels. Twenty stations recorded levels that were in their top ten lowest water levels since the last big agricultural drought in 2000. The U.S. Secretary of Agriculture designated almost all of Arkansas as being in a drought disaster. Tropical moisture began returning to Arkansas about the 6th of July. This brought higher humidities, somewhat of an increase in afternoon showers and thunderstorms, and caused the number of wildfires each day to diminish. On the 17th, the Arkansas Forestry Commission dropped the wildfire danger from Extreme to High. For the month of July, the Forestry Commission fought 430 fires, which consumed 8126 acres. A second heat wave occurred from the 17th through the 20th. During this time, numerous reporting stations rose above 100 degrees. A number of daily high temperatures records were broken, and additional daily records were broken for warmest low temperatures. The highest temperature recorded was 110 at Arkadelphia. Readings of 109 were observed at Russellville, Hot Springs airport, Cabot, and Morrilton. Stations reaching 108 included Little Rock, Mena, Dardanelle, Nimrod Dam, and Oden. Thereafter, temperatures moderated somewhat but still remained above normal. Another heat wave occurred from the 28th through the end of the month. During this period, the highest temperatures recorded were 112 at the Searcy airport, 111 at Little Rock, 110 at Cabot and Silver Hill, and 109 at Little Rock Air Force Base, the Batesville airport, Des Arc, and Evening Shade (forestry station).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of July, extreme drought covered the northwestern half of Perry County, with severe drought elsewhere. By the end of the month, conditions had worsened, with exceptional drought covering the county.
