On the 17th, the Arkansas Forestry Commission dropped the wildfire danger from Extreme to High. For the month of July, the Forestry Commission fought 430 fires, which consumed 8126 acres. A second heat wave occurred from the 17th through the 20th. During this time, numerous reporting stations rose above 100 degrees. A number of daily high temperatures records were broken, and additional daily records were broken for warmest low temperatures. The highest temperature recorded was 110 at Arkadelphia. Readings of 109 were observed at Russellville, Hot Springs airport, Cabot, and Morrilton. Stations reaching 108 included Little Rock, Mena, Dardanelle, Nimrod Dam, and Oden. Thereafter, temperatures moderated somewhat but still remained above normal. Another heat wave occurred from the 28th through the end of the month. During this period, the highest temperatures recorded were 112 at the Searcy airport, 111 at Little Rock, 110 at Cabot and Silver Hill, and 109 at Little Rock Air Force Base, the Batesville airport, Des Arc, and Evening Shade (forestry station).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of July, extreme drought extended across extreme northeastern White County, with severe drought elsewhere. By the end of the month, conditions had worsened, with exceptional drought covering the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually hot weather that began in late June continued into the first part of July, thanks to a large area of high pressure aloft. Many high temperatures of 100 degrees or higher were recorded, and a number of daily high temperature records were broken. Although enough moisture returned such that very isolated showers and thunderstorms occurred each day, the rain did little to help the drought. Early in the month, about a third of Arkansas was affected by extreme drought, with severe drought existing in almost all other parts of the state. Pastures dried up, causing many cattle growers to send their livestock to market. Some stock ponds dried up, as well. Some water utilities instituted mandatory water restrictions. The hot, dry weather contributed to the spread of wildfires. At the beginning of the month, the Arkansas Forestry Commission rated fire danger statewide as Extreme, a category which is used very rarely. County judges instituted burn bans for virtually every county in Arkansas. Sales of fireworks for the 4th of July were greatly affected, with some fireworks stands closing before the holiday. Some municipal fireworks shows were cancelled as a result of the dry weather. Dozens of fires broke out each day, but only a relatively small number of these exceeded 100 acres. On the 3rd, Arkansas Governor Mike Beebe authorized the National Guard to use Black Hawk helicopters to assist firefighters by dropping water on the larger fires using 660 gallon Bambi Buckets. On the 4th alone, the Arkansas Forestry Commission fought 43 wildfires which burned 1803 acres. During the first half of the month, many rivers in the state fell to unusually low levels. During this period, the highest temperatures recorded were 112 at the Searcy airport, 111 at Little Rock, 110 at Cabot and Silver Hill, and 109 at Little Rock Air Force Base, the Batesville airport, Des Arc, and Evening Shade (forestry station).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the beginning of July, extreme drought extended across extreme northern Woodruff County, with severe drought elsewhere. By the end of the month, conditions had worsened, with exceptional drought over most of the county except the southeast, where extreme drought existed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous, fast-moving, early autumn storm system brought severe weather to portions of the ArkLaMiss region during the evening of October 17th, 2012. The combination of intense wind shear, strong winds and adequate moisture and instability resulted in severe storms developing along and ahead of a strong cold front as it raced across the forecast area. A few reports of wind damage from bowing-type thunderstorms were received as the storms produced strong straight line winds across southeast Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down in Dermott. Many limbs were blown down, and a billboard was torn apart.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed in advance of a strong cold front on the evening of the 19th and continued into the wee hours of the 20th. Some of the storms became severe, producing damaging winds. There was one tornado as well. Just before the rain came to an end, winds brought in dust into Arkansas, causing a mud rain and dirtying thousands of cars. The dust was picked up by a dust storm in West Texas on the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Arkansas 8.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed in advance of a strong cold front on the evening of the 19th and continued into the wee hours of the 20th. Some of the storms became severe, producing damaging winds. There was one tornado as well. Just before the rain came to an end, winds brought in dust into Arkansas, causing a mud rain and dirtying thousands of cars. The dust was picked up by a dust storm in West Texas on the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon hours over portions of west central Arkansas. A few of these thunderstorms produced damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm wind blew down power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The day of the 22nd began cloudy, chilly, and foggy across most of Arkansas. During the day, however, much warmer air surged northward across much of the state. In the evening, a cold front plowed into the warmth and moisture, triggering severe thunderstorms and seven tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF1 tornado overturned an irrigation pivot and shoved it into a ditch. Some limbs were broken off in a tree line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary was draped across the Middle Red River Valley of Northeast Texas, Southeast Oklahoma and Southwest Arkansas during the afternoon and evening hours of August 5th.  Moderate instability was present across the region with temperatures near 100 degrees and with ample moisture in place...scattered showers and thunderstorms developed across the Four State Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed near Kiblah, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Arkansas on the 7th, causing numerous severe thunderstorms to occur. Wind damage was widespread. Approximately 80,000 electric customers lost service; this was about four times the number of outages that Tropical Depression Isaac produced in Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although the remnants of Isaac were centered over Missouri on the 1st, showers and thunderstorms spiraling in toward Isaac's center affected most of Arkansas on the 1st and into the wee hours of the 2nd.  Isaac had been a hurricane in the Gulf of Mexico, but had weakened to a tropical depression by the time it reached Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front pushed north across the region and eventually stalled across northern sections of the Mid-South by the late afternoon on June 3, 2012.  An upper level disturbance moving through triggered numerous showers and thunderstorms along the warm front and eventually formed a mesoscale convective system that pushed through during the evening hours of June 3, 2012.  There were reports of damaging winds and large hail.  After midnight additional showers and thunderstorms redeveloped along the stalled front producing a few more reports of damaging winds during the early morning hours of June 4, 2012.
A cold front moved into Arkansas on the 5th, sparking a number of severe thunderstorms. On the 6th and 7th, the front had weakened but remained in the state, setting off a few more severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down at Hollywood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into Arkansas on the 5th, sparking a number of severe thunderstorms. On the 6th and 7th, the front had weakened but remained in the state, setting off a few more severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Arkansas 160. Power outages occurred in Hermitage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An MCS developed across Southeast Texas during the overnight and early morning hours of April 2nd. This upper level disturbance rounded the base of an upper level trough across Southeast Texas and moved northeastward into Northeast Texas, Northern Louisiana and Southern Arkansas during the late morning and afternoon hours of April 2nd. The atmosphere ahead of this line of thunderstorms became very unstable by late morning with CAPE values approaching 4000 J/Kg. Very steep lapse rates were also present such that large hail and damaging thunderstorm wind gusts were the primary threat with these storms. Widespread tree damage was reported across all of Northeast Texas, Northern Louisiana and Southern Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were snapped and uprooted in the Parkers Chapel community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure aloft moving from northwest to southeast set off thunderstorms in parts of northern and eastern Arkansas on the morning of the 24th.

A slow moving cold front pushed into the region during the late afternoon and evening of August 4th. Thunderstorms developed along this boundary as it moved into a very warm and humid air mass that was in place over northwestern Arkansas. Damaging winds occurred with the stronger thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts to 81 mph were measured by instrumentation located at the Cooper Elementary School on Highway 71.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of scattered thunderstorms developed across the Middle Red River Valley of Northeast Texas and Southwest Arkansas during the late night hours of October 13 and the predawn hours of October 14th.  The thunderstorms developed in an unstable airmass and were preceded by a strong cold front in association with an upper level trough moving into the Southern Plains and Lower Mississippi Valley.  Strong damaging wind gusts were the primary result with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least four trees were reported downed on the wide side of Foreman, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall, exceeding 3 to 6 inches across some areas, caused widespread minor river flooding across Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain at Batesville and upstream along the White River caused the White River at Batesville to crest just below moderate flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed in advance of a strong cold front on the evening of the 19th and continued into the wee hours of the 20th. Some of the storms became severe, producing damaging winds. There was one tornado as well. Just before the rain came to an end, winds brought in dust into Arkansas, causing a mud rain and dirtying thousands of cars. The dust was picked up by a dust storm in West Texas on the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter estimated wind gusts reached 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front near the Arkansas/Missouri border helped to trigger a few severe thunderstorms on the 5th and 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were uprooted, and tree tops and limbs were blown down. Two buildings were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the Southern Plains and moved to the northeast. The strong low pressure system had moved to the Kansas and Missouri border by the time the event started. A very strong low-level jet, along with a very strong jet stream, moved across the Mid-South during the evening hours. An upper level short wave moved across Arkansas during the early evening hours. This short wave was the focus of thunderstorm development which produced severe thunderstorms over parts of East Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell in Marmaduke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although the remnants of Isaac were centered over Missouri on the 1st, showers and thunderstorms spiraling in toward Isaac's center affected most of Arkansas on the 1st and into the wee hours of the 2nd.  Isaac had been a hurricane in the Gulf of Mexico, but had weakened to a tropical depression by the time it reached Arkansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down onto Arkansas 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The high pressure aloft that had been over Arkansas since June finally drifted to the west. This allowed some tropical moisture to return to the state. Scattered thunderstorms developed on the 6th, 7th, and 8th, and some of these storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two barns were blown down, some outbuildings were damaged, and trees were blown down onto roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours of the 19th over portions of northwestern Arkansas. A few of these thunderstorms produced damaging wind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm wind blew down large tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed once again across the region during the afternoon of June 15th.  These thunderstorms developed under a very unstable airmass and weak capping in the atmosphere.  Very high lapse rates were present throughout the atmosphere as well which was prone for the storms to produce large hail.  Damaging winds also accompanied the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed near Foreman, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on the 7th, thunderstorms formed in a warm, humid air mass in advance of a cold front. Isolated severe weather and locally heavy rain occurred. The storms diminished quickly by midday. However, by late afternoon, isolated severe storms redeveloped in western Arkansas. These storms moved off to the southeast and continued into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arkansas 14 was covered by water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The high pressure aloft that had been over Arkansas since June finally drifted to the west. This allowed some tropical moisture to return to the state. Scattered thunderstorms developed on the 6th, 7th, and 8th, and some of these storms became severe.

The high pressure aloft that had been over Arkansas since June finally drifted to the west. This allowed some tropical moisture to return to the state. Scattered thunderstorms developed on the 6th, 7th, and 8th, and some of these storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Collietown Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very slow-moving upper level low pressure center brought severe weather, heavy rain, and flash flooding from the 20th through the 22nd. For the 24-hour period ending at 7 AM on the 21st, some of the larger rainfall amounts included 6.20 inches at Norfork (Baxter Co.), 6.10 inches at Mountain Home (Baxter Co.), 5.88 inches at Damascus (Van Buren Co.), 5.47 inches at Maumelle (Pulaski Co.), 4.90 inches at Clarkridge (Baxter Co.), 4.76 inches at Sherwood (Pulaski Co.), 4.45 inches at Sheridan (Grant Co.), 4.42 inches at Crystal Valley (Pulaski Co.), 4.38 inches at North Little Rock (Pulaski Co.), 4.35 inches at Calico Rock (Izard Co.), 4.35 inches at Ivan (Dallas Co.), 4.33 inches at Camden (Ouachita Co.), 4.27 inches at Conway (Faulkner Co.), 4.25 inches at Little Rock (Pulaski Co.), 4.10 inches at Moro Bay (Bradley Co.), and 4.07 inches at Mountain View (Stone Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flooding along the South Fork of the Spring River. On the 24th at the Kia Kima Scout Reservation, a 4 year-old boy was helping clean up the area.  According to news reports, a group of older boys left the clean-up site to return to the camping area.  Sometime thereafter, the younger boy asked if he could catch up with the other boys.  At some point, the boy ended up drowning in the river, which was described as high and rolling.  His body was recovered on the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level trough across the western plains lifted north and east towards the region during the afternoon and overnight hours of April 3rd and morning hours of April 4th. Deep layer shear supported the development of super cell thunderstorms across North Central Texas during the afternoon hours of April 3rd and these storms moved into the region, producing strong and damaging wind gusts, large hail and a few tornadoes across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Lisbon Road northwest of El Dorado, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms formed around the base of the upper low that was spinning over Eastern Oklahoma and Western Arkansas across a surface boundary.  Large hail occurred with the storms that developed across Southern Arkansas and Northern Louisiana.

Unseasonably hot temperatures occurred on the 29th, with many areas reaching the mid to upper 90s. The hot weather helped spark an isolated thunderstorm in Saline County during the afternoon. This storm tracked into Hot Spring County, bringing numerous reports of hail and a few of wind damage. The storm weakened over Dallas County early in the evening.

Unseasonably hot temperatures occurred on the 29th, with many areas reaching the mid to upper 90s. The hot weather helped spark an isolated thunderstorm in Saline County during the afternoon. This storm tracked into Hot Spring County, bringing numerous reports of hail and a few of wind damage. The storm weakened over Dallas County early in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many trees were blown down in Malvern, according to the sheriff's office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough rapidly traversed east across Oklahoma, Arkansas, and extreme Northeast Texas on February 1st, just ahead of a weak cold front that extended from North Texas, into Southeast Oklahoma and Western Arkansas.  The air mass south of the front over Northeast Texas, Southwest Arkansas, and extreme Northern Louisiana destabilized sufficiently as temperatures warmed well into the 70s, within a very moist air mass in place.  Cold temperatures and strong instability aloft ahead of this shortwave trough resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms developing over the region, some of which produced marginally severe to at times, severe hail.  These storms exited the region during the late afternoon hours, although additional widely scattered thunderstorms redeveloped during the mid-evening hours over Northwest and Northcentral Louisiana, and again produced a couple of reports of marginally severe hail in and near Monroe, LA.  These storms diminished during the late evening, as they moved away from the better forcing near the front as it drifted into extreme Northern Louisiana and Deep East Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail reported in Fouke, Arkansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very slow-moving upper level low pressure center brought severe weather, heavy rain, and flash flooding from the 20th through the 22nd. For the 24-hour period ending at 7 AM on the 21st, some of the larger rainfall amounts included 6.20 inches at Norfork (Baxter Co.), 6.10 inches at Mountain Home (Baxter Co.), 5.88 inches at Damascus (Van Buren Co.), 5.47 inches at Maumelle (Pulaski Co.), 4.90 inches at Clarkridge (Baxter Co.), 4.76 inches at Sherwood (Pulaski Co.), 4.45 inches at Sheridan (Grant Co.), 4.42 inches at Crystal Valley (Pulaski Co.), 4.38 inches at North Little Rock (Pulaski Co.), 4.35 inches at Calico Rock (Izard Co.), 4.35 inches at Ivan (Dallas Co.), 4.33 inches at Camden (Ouachita Co.), 4.27 inches at Conway (Faulkner Co.), 4.25 inches at Little Rock (Pulaski Co.), 4.10 inches at Moro Bay (Bradley Co.), and 4.07 inches at Mountain View (Stone Co.).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto Angora Mountain Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late afternoon thunderstorms occurred across parts of west-central Arkansas, aided by intense surface heating and a weak upper level disturbance passing over the region. There was an isolated occurrence of damaging winds resulting from a decaying thunderstorm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief downburst winds from a decaying thunderstorm caused damage to a barn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several heavy rain events from January 23rd through January 27th pushed portions of the White, Cache and Petit Jean Rivers above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of heavy rain events in late January pushed the Cache River at Patterson above flood stage in late January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The approach of a cold front and very hot weather over Arkansas contributed to the development of a few severe thunderstorms on the afternoon and evening of the 19th. As the front moved through the state on the 20th and 21st, additional isolated severe storms formed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto Arkansas 54, blocking one lane.
